title: POPE: Privileged On-Policy Exploration for Hard Reasoning
text:
Authors: Yuxiao Qu, Amrith Setlur, Virginia Smith, Ruslan Salakhutdinov, Aviral Kumar (CMU)
Link: arXiv
Read date: 2026-03-05

title: POPE: Privileged On-Policy Exploration for Hard Reasoning
text:
Authors: Yuxiao Qu, Amrith Setlur, Virginia Smith, Ruslan Salakhutdinov, Aviral Kumar (CMU)
Link: arXiv
Read date: 2026-03-05

title: POPE: Privileged On-Policy Exploration for Hard Reasoning
text:
Summary
Standard on-policy RL for LLM reasoning fails on hard problems because the model never samples a correct solution, yielding zero reward and no learning signal. POPE (Privileged On-Policy Exploration) addresses this by using oracle solutions as privileged information—not as distillation targets, but as exploration guides. Short prefixes from oracle solutions are prepended to hard problems with instructions to continue solving, enabling non-zero rewards during training. The key insight is that learned behaviors transfer back to unguided problems through a “stitching and overlap” mechanism: guided rollouts steer the model into favorable internal states, and natural chain-of-thought backtracking expands coverage to nearby states reachable without guidance. The paper also identifies “ray interference” as a failure mode when mixing easy and hard problems, where optimization preferentially advances already-solvable problems.

title: POPE: Privileged On-Policy Exploration for Hard Reasoning
text:
Key Ideas

Zero-reward collapse on hard problems — On-policy RL (e.g. GRPO) produces no learning signal when the model can’t sample any correct solution; entropy bonuses and higher clip ratios don’t fix this and introduce pathologies.
Ray interference — Mixing easy and hard problems during training is counterproductive; optimization preferentially improves already-solvable problems while hard-problem performance stalls.
Privileged guidance ≠ distillation — Rather than SFT on oracle solutions (which causes catastrophic performance drops of ~85%), POPE uses short oracle prefixes to steer exploration while preserving the model’s own reasoning structure.
Stitching and overlap transfer — Guided rollouts place the model in favorable states; natural backtracking during chain-of-thought extends coverage to unguided-reachable states, enabling transfer without explicit curriculum.
1:1 guided/unguided mixture — Training on equal parts guided and unguided versions of hard problems avoids ray interference and enables stable learning.

title: POPE: Privileged On-Policy Exploration for Hard Reasoning
text:
Method

Prefix selection: Identify the shortest oracle solution prefix that enables at least one successful completion from the current policy.
Guided rollouts: Augment hard problems with the selected prefix plus a system instruction to continue solving from where the prefix leaves off.
Training mixture: 1:1 ratio of guided and unguided versions of hard problems. Easy problems can optionally be mixed in without ray interference (unlike vanilla RL).
RL algorithm: Standard on-policy RL (GRPO-style); the innovation is entirely in the exploration strategy, not the optimization.
Transfer mechanism: The model learns reasoning patterns in guided settings that naturally transfer because chain-of-thought backtracking revisits earlier reasoning steps, creating overlap between guided and unguided state distributions.

title: POPE: Privileged On-Policy Exploration for Hard Reasoning
text:
Results

Hard-problem pass@16: ~29% improvement over baseline RL on training-set hard problems.
AIME 2025: 58% pass@1, 83% pass@16 (vs. 48%/77% baseline).
HMMT 2025: 40% pass@1, 70% pass@16 (substantial gains on harder benchmark).
SFT on full oracle solutions: Catastrophic ~85% performance drop, validating that distillation is not the answer.
Ablation (no backtracking): Modified system instructions discouraging revisiting guidance reduced transfer, confirming the stitching mechanism.

title: POPE: Privileged On-Policy Exploration for Hard Reasoning
text:
Strengths

Clean identification of a fundamental failure mode (zero-reward collapse) with systematic analysis of why standard fixes fail.
Principled and practical: minimal changes to standard RL pipelines; only modifies the data/exploration, not the optimizer.
Strong empirical results on competitive math benchmarks (AIME, HMMT).
Good ablations validating the transfer mechanism (backtracking intervention, SFT comparison).
Identifies ray interference as a distinct failure mode with clear experimental evidence.

title: POPE: Privileged On-Policy Exploration for Hard Reasoning
text:
Weaknesses / Limitations

Requires access to oracle solutions for hard problems—limits applicability to domains without ground-truth answers.
Prefix selection strategy (shortest prefix enabling success) may be sensitive to the current policy’s capability; unclear how robust this is across training.
Evaluated primarily on math reasoning; transfer to other reasoning domains (code, science) not demonstrated.
The stitching/overlap explanation is intuitive but not formally characterized; hard to predict when transfer will or won’t work.
Comparison limited to GRPO-style RL; unclear how POPE interacts with other RL algorithms (PPO, DPO variants).

title: POPE: Privileged On-Policy Exploration for Hard Reasoning
text:
Relevance to My Work

Directly relevant to GRPO work — POPE addresses exactly the failure mode of on-policy RL on hard problems. If training GRPO on challenging tasks with sparse rewards, POPE’s prefix-guided exploration could be a practical solution.
Curriculum design — The ray interference finding is important: naively mixing easy and hard problems hurts. This should inform how we construct training mixtures.
Self-distillation connections — POPE is a middle ground between pure RL and distillation. Connects to ideas about using privileged information during training that gets removed at test time.
Exploration in RL for LLMs — Complements work on reward shaping and search-based methods (MCTS distillation); POPE is simpler but requires oracle access.

title: POPE: Privileged On-Policy Exploration for Hard Reasoning
text:
Key Takeaways

On-policy RL for LLMs has a hard floor: if the model can’t sample any correct solution, no amount of entropy bonuses or clipping fixes help.
Mixing easy and hard problems causes ray interference—optimization takes the path of least resistance toward already-solvable problems.
Oracle prefixes as exploration guides (not distillation targets) enable learning on hard problems while preserving the model’s reasoning structure.
Transfer from guided to unguided settings works through chain-of-thought backtracking, which creates state-space overlap between the two settings.
POPE is a practical, minimal-change addition to standard RL pipelines that expands the frontier of learnable problems.

title: DreamZero: World Action Models are Zero-shot Policies
text:
Authors: Seonghyeon Ye, Yunhao Ge, Kaiyuan Zheng, Shenyuan Gao, Sihyun Yu, et al. (NVIDIA)
Link: arXiv | Project | GitHub
Read date: 2026-03-01

title: DreamZero: World Action Models are Zero-shot Policies
text:
Authors: Seonghyeon Ye, Yunhao Ge, Kaiyuan Zheng, Shenyuan Gao, Sihyun Yu, et al. (NVIDIA)
Link: arXiv | Project | GitHub
Read date: 2026-03-01

title: DreamZero: World Action Models are Zero-shot Policies
text:
Summary
VLAs (Vision-Language-Action models) generalize well to new objects and semantics but struggle with novel physical motions and environments—they lack spatiotemporal priors from image-text pretraining. DreamZero introduces a World Action Model (WAM) built on a pretrained 14B autoregressive video diffusion backbone. By jointly predicting future video frames and actions, DreamZero learns physical dynamics via an inverse-dynamics formulation: align motor commands with predicted visual futures. This shifts action learning from dense state-action imitation to world-model-guided control. Key outcomes: >2× improvement over SOTA VLAs on environment/task generalization, effective learning from diverse heterogeneous robot data (no repetitive demos), real-time control at 7Hz via 38× inference speedup, and two forms of cross-embodiment transfer—video-only demonstrations (+42% with 10–20 min) and few-shot embodiment adaptation (30 min play data to transfer AgiBot G1 → YAM).

title: DreamZero: World Action Models are Zero-shot Policies
text:
Key Ideas

VLAs lack spatiotemporal priors — VLM backbones use static image-text; VLAs fail at novel skills (e.g. “untie shoelace”) not in robot data. Video diffusion encodes how the world evolves.
Joint video-action = inverse dynamics — DreamZero decomposes as \(\pi(\mathbf{o}_{l:l+H},\mathbf{a}_{l:l+H}) = \pi(\mathbf{o}|\text{context})\,\pi(\mathbf{a}|\mathbf{o},\text{context})\). Video prediction is implicit visual planning; actions follow predicted futures.
Diverse data > repetitive demos — Heterogeneous trajectories outperform same-hours multi-task repetitive data. Generalist policies don’t need many demos per task.
Autoregressive + KV-cache reset — After each action chunk, replace predicted frames with ground-truth observations in KV cache to prevent error accumulation; keeps native frame rate for precise video-action alignment.
Policy quality ∝ video quality — Larger pretrained video models → better video predictions → better action execution. Improving robotics reduces to improving video generation.
Cross-embodiment transfer — Video-only from humans or other robots (no actions) improves target robot by >42% with 10–20 min. Few-shot adaptation: 30 min play on new embodiment preserves zero-shot generalization.

title: DreamZero: World Action Models are Zero-shot Policies
text:
Method

Architecture: 14B autoregressive DiT (flow matching) on pretrained video diffusion (Team Wan 2025). Inputs: visual context (VAE), language (text encoder), proprioception (state encoder). Joint video + action prediction via separate decoders; minimal added parameters.
Formulation: Chunk-wise autoregressive prediction. Each chunk: \(K\) latent frames, horizon \(H\). Joint flow-matching objective over video latents \(\mathbf{z}_t\) and normalized actions \(\mathbf{a}_t\). Teacher forcing: denoise chunk \(k\) conditioned on clean chunks \(1..k{-}1\).
Training objective (Eq. 3): \(\mathcal{L} = \mathbb{E}[\sum_k w(t_k) \| \mathbf{u}_\theta([\mathbf{z}_{t_k}^k,\mathbf{a}_{t_k}^k]; \mathcal{C}_k, \mathbf{c}, \mathbf{q}_k, t_k) - \mathbf{v}^k \|^2]\), with velocity \(\mathbf{v}^k = [\mathbf{z}_1^k,\mathbf{a}_1^k] - [\mathbf{z}_0^k,\mathbf{a}_0^k]\).
Shared timestep for video and action (unlike some WAMs) for faster convergence.
Inference optimizations: DreamZero-Flash (decoupled denoising schedules), system-level parallelism/caching, quantization, CUDA tuning → 38× speedup, 7Hz closed-loop.

title: DreamZero: World Action Models are Zero-shot Policies
text:
Results

Environment & task generalization: >2× average task progress vs SOTA pretrained VLAs (RoboArena, PolaRiS). Retains +10% after task-specific post-training.
Real-time control: 7Hz action chunk generation on 14B model via 38× inference speedup.
Video-only cross-embodiment: Human (12 min) or YAM robot (20 min) video demos → +42% relative improvement on AgiBot G1 unseen tasks.
Few-shot embodiment adaptation: Pretrained on AgiBot G1, adapt to YAM with 30 min play data; retains zero-shot generalization.
Data: ~500 hours real-world; shows non-trivial Genie Sim 3.0 (100 tasks) without sim training.

title: DreamZero: World Action Models are Zero-shot Policies
text:
Strengths

Strong empirical gains on generalization; breaks VLA ceiling for novel skills and environments.
Data efficiency: diverse heterogeneous data suffices; no need for repeated demonstrations per task.
Novel cross-embodiment results: video-only transfer and 30-min few-shot adaptation are striking.
Real-time deployable (7Hz) despite 14B diffusion model—important system contribution.
Open-source: weights, inference code, benchmarks (RoboArena, PolaRiS, Genie Sim 3.0).
Clean formulation: policy quality tied to video quality; clear research direction.

title: DreamZero: World Action Models are Zero-shot Policies
text:
Weaknesses / Limitations

Single embodiment focus (AgiBot G1, YAM); broader cross-embodiment scaling not shown.
500 hours of real data still nontrivial; cost of data collection unclear.
Video diffusion backbone dependency; scaling/generalization may plateau with video model quality.
Limited comparison to other WAMs (Kim et al., Liang et al., Pai et al.) in main text; ablations could be deeper.
7Hz is usable but not high-frequency; some manipulation may need higher rates.

title: DreamZero: World Action Models are Zero-shot Policies
text:
Relevance to My Work
How does this connect to MedEnv, GRPO, or other active projects?

World models for RL/planning: If MedEnv or similar settings involve sequential decision-making, WAMs offer a different backbone than pure policy nets—video as implicit planner, inverse dynamics for actions. Could inform hierarchical or model-based RL.
Data efficiency: DreamZero’s diverse-data story (vs repeated demos) parallels sample-efficiency concerns in RL; lessons may transfer to how we structure demonstrations or play data.
Cross-domain transfer: Video-only and few-shot adaptation could inspire transfer setups in non-robotics domains (e.g. sim→real, domain A→domain B) if we have video-like sequential observations.
Foundation models for control: Contrast with language/LLM-based control; video diffusion as alternative pretrained prior for embodied AI.

title: DreamZero: World Action Models are Zero-shot Policies
text:
Key Takeaways

WAMs built on video diffusion beat VLAs on motion/skill generalization by inheriting spatiotemporal priors; joint video-action prediction = inverse dynamics.
Diverse heterogeneous robot data outperforms repetitive demos; generalist policies don’t require many demos per task.
Cross-embodiment transfer is surprisingly efficient: video-only (10–20 min) and 30-min few-shot adaptation both work.
Policy performance is tied to video generation quality; improving robotics = improving video models.
38× inference speedup (decoupled schedules, caching, quantization) makes 14B diffusion viable for 7Hz real-time control.

title: LLMs Can Learn to Reason Via Off-Policy RL (OAPL)
text:
Authors: Daniel Ritter, Owen Oertell, Bradley Guo, Jonathan Chang, Kianté Brantley, Wen Sun (Cornell, Databricks, Harvard)
Link: arXiv
Read date: 2026-03-01

title: LLMs Can Learn to Reason Via Off-Policy RL (OAPL)
text:
Authors: Daniel Ritter, Owen Oertell, Bradley Guo, Jonathan Chang, Kianté Brantley, Wen Sun (Cornell, Databricks, Harvard)
Link: arXiv
Read date: 2026-03-01

title: LLMs Can Learn to Reason Via Off-Policy RL (OAPL)
text:
Summary
Modern RL post-training (e.g. GRPO) assumes on-policy data, but in practice the inference engine (vLLM) and trainer (HuggingFace) often disagree on log-probs or run asynchronously, making data effectively off-policy. Prior work either adds importance sampling (high variance) or modifies the inference engine (slow, doesn’t fix async lag). OAPL embraces off-policyness: it formulates a KL-regularized objective where the KL is to the lagged inference policy \(\pi_{\text{vllm}}\), derives a closed-form optimal policy, and trains with a simple squared regression loss that fits \(\beta \ln(\pi/\pi_{\text{vllm}})\) to the optimal advantage. No importance weights, no clipping. Sync trainer and inference infrequently (e.g. every 50–400 steps); train off-policy in between. Outperforms GRPO+IS on math (AIME, HMMT, BRUMO), matches DeepCoder on LiveCodeBench with ~3× fewer samples, and scales better on Pass@k.

title: LLMs Can Learn to Reason Via Off-Policy RL (OAPL)
text:
Key Ideas

On-policy is not necessary — off-policy methods (DDPG, SAC) often beat PPO/REINFORCE in classic RL; same applies to LLM post-training.
KL to \(\pi_{\text{vllm}}\) instead of \(\pi_{\text{old}}\) — GRPO clips \(\pi/\pi_{\text{old}}\) but the first gradient can already push \(\pi\) far away; OAPL explicitly regularizes against the sampling policy.
Closed-form optimum — \(\pi^\star(y|x) \propto \pi_{\text{vllm}}(y|x) \exp(r/\beta)\), so \(\beta \ln(\pi^\star/\pi_{\text{vllm}}) = A^\star = r - V^\star\).
\(\hat{V}^\star\) from group rollouts — estimate \(V^\star(x) = \beta \ln \frac{1}{G}\sum_i \exp(r(x,y_i)/\beta)\) using rollouts from \(\pi_{\text{vllm}}\); no separate critic.
Squared regression loss — minimize \(\sum_{x,i} (\beta \ln \frac{\pi(y_i|x)}{\pi_{\text{vllm}}(y_i|x)} - (r(x,y_i) - \hat{V}^\star(x)))^2\). Same minimizer regardless of sampling distribution.

title: LLMs Can Learn to Reason Via Off-Policy RL (OAPL)
text:
Method

Objective (Eq. 1): \(\max_\pi \mathbb{E}_{y \sim \pi} r(x,y) - \beta \text{KL}(\pi \| \pi_{\text{vllm}})\) — maximize reward, stay close to inference policy.
Optimal advantage: \(A^\star = r - V^\star\), with \(V^\star = \beta \ln \mathbb{E}_{y \sim \pi_{\text{vllm}}} \exp(r/\beta)\).
Algorithm: Sync \(\pi\) and \(\pi_{\text{vllm}}\); generate rollouts from \(\pi_{\text{vllm}}\); add to buffer; train \(\pi\) on Eq. 3; every \(L\) steps, sync and clear buffer.
Two betas: \(\beta_1\) for \(\hat{V}^\star\) (Eq. 2), \(\beta_2\) for the loss (Eq. 3). \(\beta_1=1\), \(\beta_2=1e-3\) worked well.
Policy lag \(L\): Math: 50 or 100; code: ~400 gradient steps per sync.

title: LLMs Can Learn to Reason Via Off-Policy RL (OAPL)
text:
Results

Math (Qwen3-4B-Thinking): OAPL outperforms GRPO+IS on AIME-25, HMMT-25 (Feb/Nov), BRUMO-25 across Pass@1/5/10.
Code (DeepSeek-R1-Distill-Qwen-14B): Matches DeepCoder on LiveCodeBench Pass@k with ~200K vs ~650K training samples (~3× fewer).
Entropy: OAPL preserves entropy; GRPO collapses it. OAPL scales better on Pass@k for \(k=1..256\).
Robustness: Stable with \(L=100\) (math) and \(L\approx 400\) (code) — 100× more off-policy than prior IS-based work.

title: LLMs Can Learn to Reason Via Off-Policy RL (OAPL)
text:
Strengths

Principled off-policy objective with no importance sampling; avoids variance and heuristics (clip ratios, delete tokens).
Simple squared regression; no critic, no extra value network.
Fully asynchronous between syncs; reuse data, fewer generations.
Empirically: better stability, entropy, and Pass@k scaling than GRPO.
Directly addresses trainer–inference mismatch (FP differences, async pipelines).

title: LLMs Can Learn to Reason Via Off-Policy RL (OAPL)
text:
Weaknesses / Limitations

Two-stage code setup filters prompts with no correct solutions; may not reflect real online training.
\(\beta_1,\beta_2\) swept for math but not for code; sensitivity under-studied.
Compared mainly to GRPO+IS; fewer comparisons to REBEL, REFUEL, AGRO, or other squared-loss methods.
No ablations on \(L\); unclear sweet spot for different scales/tasks.

title: LLMs Can Learn to Reason Via Off-Policy RL (OAPL)
text:
Relevance to My Work

MedEnv / GRPO: Same verifiable-reward setting (math correctness, code execution). If our trainer and inference diverge (async, different backends), OAPL could be a drop-in alternative to GRPO+IS.
Sample efficiency: 3× fewer generations for similar performance — matters for expensive clinical trajectory rollouts.
Entropy and Pass@k: If we care about Pass@k for clinical plan generation (sample \(k\) plans, take best), OAPL’s better scaling is directly relevant.
No importance weights: Simpler, more stable than adding IS to GRPO when we have policy lag.

title: LLMs Can Learn to Reason Via Off-Policy RL (OAPL)
text:
Key Takeaways

Off-policy RL is viable and often preferable for LLM post-training when trainer and inference disagree or lag.
KL to \(\pi_{\text{vllm}}\) + squared regression to \(A^\star\) yields a simple, stable objective.
Infrequent sync (\(L=50\)–400) + explicit KL regularization preserves entropy and improves Pass@k scaling vs GRPO.
OAPL matches or beats GRPO with ~3× fewer training samples on code.

title: PipelineRL: Faster On-policy RL for Long Sequence Generation
text:
Authors: Alexandre Piché, Ehsan Kamalloo, Rafael Pardinas, Xiaoyin Chen, Dzmitry Bahdanau
Link: arXiv
Read date: 2026-03-03

title: PipelineRL: Faster On-policy RL for Long Sequence Generation
text:
Authors: Alexandre Piché, Ehsan Kamalloo, Rafael Pardinas, Xiaoyin Chen, Dzmitry Bahdanau
Link: arXiv
Read date: 2026-03-03

title: PipelineRL: Faster On-policy RL for Long Sequence Generation
text:
Summary
Scaling RL for LLMs faces a fundamental tension: synchronous (conventional) RL keeps data on-policy but wastes GPU cycles waiting for generation to finish, while naive asynchronous RL improves throughput but produces stale off-policy data that harms algorithms like GRPO and PPO. PipelineRL resolves this by running generation, preprocessing, and training as concurrent pipeline stages with a novel in-flight weight update mechanism—the generation engine briefly pauses mid-sequence to receive fresh weights over high-speed interconnect, then continues generating. This keeps early tokens slightly stale but recent tokens fresh, achieving ~2x faster learning on math reasoning tasks (128 H100 GPUs) while maintaining on-policy data quality comparable to synchronous baselines.

title: PipelineRL: Faster On-policy RL for Long Sequence Generation
text:
Key Ideas

The synchronous RL bottleneck — Conventional RL alternates between generation and training; GPUs sit idle during the other phase. Throughput is fundamentally limited by the slower stage.
In-flight weight updates — Instead of waiting for all sequences to finish before updating weights, PipelineRL pushes new weights to the generation engine mid-generation. The engine briefly pauses, loads weights, and continues generating in-progress sequences with the fresh model.
Three-stage pipeline — Actor (vLLM generation) → Preprocessor (reference log-probs) → Trainer (DeepSpeed), connected via Redis. Stages run concurrently to maximize utilization.
On-policyness via Effective Sample Size (ESS) — Despite higher maximum token lag, PipelineRL’s ESS matches synchronous RL with G=8 rollouts, because recent tokens (which matter most) use fresh weights.
Importance-weighted REINFORCE — Uses truncated importance weights (c=5) to handle the mild off-policyness from in-flight updates.

title: PipelineRL: Faster On-policy RL for Long Sequence Generation
text:
Method

Architecture: Distributed system with separate GPU pools for generation (48 GPUs, vLLM) and training (80 GPUs, DeepSpeed). Redis for inter-stage data streaming. Online sequence packing and ring buffers minimize lag.
In-flight mechanism: Generation engine receives weight updates via high-speed inter-accelerator network with minimal pause. KV cache from old weights is retained (recomputation possible but trades throughput).
RL algorithm: REINFORCE with importance sampling and weight clamping. Compatible with PPO/GRPO-style algorithms.
Batch configuration: Effective batch size 4096, compared against conventional RL with G=8 and G=16 rollouts per prompt.

title: PipelineRL: Faster On-policy RL for Long Sequence Generation
text:
Results

~2x faster learning compared to conventional RL baselines on long-form math reasoning.
MATH500: 84.6% (PipelineRL batch 4096) vs ~82% (OpenReasoner Zero baseline).
AIME2024: 19.8% (PipelineRL) vs ~20% (baseline) — matches performance at 2x the speed.
On-policyness: ESS comparable to conventional G=8, significantly better than fully asynchronous approaches.
Hardware: 128 H100 GPUs across 16 DGX nodes.

title: PipelineRL: Faster On-policy RL for Long Sequence Generation
text:
Strengths

Clean identification of the throughput vs. on-policyness trade-off as the core scaling bottleneck for LLM RL.
In-flight weight updates are a simple, elegant mechanism—minimal engineering complexity for significant gains.
Thorough analysis of on-policyness (ESS metrics, token-level lag analysis).
Open-source implementation; modular design separating generation/training infrastructure.
Practical: 2x speedup directly translates to halving RL training costs.

title: PipelineRL: Faster On-policy RL for Long Sequence Generation
text:
Weaknesses / Limitations

Gains diminish when sequence lengths are uniform (no variance to exploit in pipeline scheduling).
Stale KV cache from old weights is a theoretical concern; impact not fully characterized.
Results limited to math reasoning; unclear how gains transfer to other RL domains (code, dialogue, tool use).
128 H100 GPU setup is not accessible to most researchers; unclear how it scales down.
Early tokens in sequences still carry high lag; practical impact on learning signal unclear.

title: PipelineRL: Faster On-policy RL for Long Sequence Generation
text:
Relevance to My Work

Direct relevance to GRPO work — PipelineRL explicitly addresses off-policyness issues that affect GRPO. If scaling GRPO training, this pipeline architecture could be the infrastructure layer.
Systems perspective on RL scaling — Complements algorithmic improvements (reward shaping, self-distillation) with systems-level gains; both are needed for practical large-scale RL.
Importance sampling connection — The truncated importance weights approach could inform how to handle off-policy corrections in other settings (e.g., self-distillation with delayed model updates).

title: PipelineRL: Faster On-policy RL for Long Sequence Generation
text:
Key Takeaways

The main bottleneck in scaling LLM RL is the synchronous generation-training loop, not the algorithm itself.
In-flight weight updates elegantly resolve the throughput vs. on-policyness trade-off—push fresh weights mid-generation rather than waiting for completion.
~2x faster learning with maintained data quality; directly halves compute cost for RL training.
On-policyness can be measured via ESS; PipelineRL stays close to synchronous baselines despite asynchronous execution.
Open-source modular implementation makes this practically adoptable for scaled RL training.

Let the Policy Judge Itself: Grounded Self-Distillation Reward Models

Current idea we have been iterating on.

Policy as a Judge (POJ).
Variants:

Self-prompting: llm-as-a-judge but the llm is the policy itself, conditioned on the environment feedback.
Self-reward mode: attach a latent reward head that outputs a continious valued scalar (0, 1) as the reward. conditioned on the feedback when doing the reward calculation pass.
add in the calibration and l_pred losses to maintain teacher stability: possibly just an ablation. Should start without this. Might not even need it at first.
self-reward hack detection: instead of training the latent head to output a reward value, train it to output a hack detection classification



Self-Incrimination // Adverserial Reward Hack Detection
2-3 variants:

Self-prompting/distillation judging: condition on the feedback/verified output and prompt self-llm if it was a reward hack or not.
self-reward hack detection: instead of training the latent head to output a reward value as in POJ (see above), train it to output a hack detection classification.
adverserial two player game setup: exact same setup as in our earlier proposed ARHD system but the labels y^* that we needed come from the two above approaches (self-prompting and self-reward hack model). This is different in that we add include the adverserial objective to the loss. Hence, the minimax game/adverserial.



Dynamic reward sparsity for RLVR

Right now, RLVR gives 1 bit of information. SDPO proposed token-level distillation which theoretically is the upper bound on the information that can be given. However, sometimes this isn’t always a good thing. What if the self-teacher is wrong? or what if it leads to a non-elegant, underoptimized solution? There are many different reasons for why we don’t want perfect imitation of the teacher. Sometimes noise is helpful. You don’t want to get stuck optimizing in a contained zone of the reward/solution space. Could be better zones in the reward landscape. Thus, ideally, you want some balance between complete sparsity and density.
This can help do things like

preserve diversity of solution space, which is measurable by plotting the t-sne of the reward clusters, analyzing pass@k performance in relation to pass@1… should hopefully go up as well


smooth out the optimization process


lead to quicker convergence


theory: if rewards are sparse/1 bit only, then it will take longer for the policy to fizzle into the correct part of the solutuon space. it will neeed to do more exploration, with lots of noise. Thus, varying the bits-per-rollout ratio can potentially lead to quciker convergence to the correct zones.




Population-based RLVR for Inducing Exploration

Right now, RLVR is done with one seed, one policy but what if you can run n different seeds/variants of the policy at once? They may all end up at different zones in the reward space. This can help improve the diversity of solutions and also induce more exploration.
Can potentially help solve the reweight vs. explore debate (i.e., is rlvr just sharpening the distribution already present in the base model or is it exploring fundamentally new solutions).

Scaling environment time compute: In real life, evolution works very similar to RLVR but there is a key difference: the environment also evolves and changes. The reward signals shift. In RLVR, the reward/verifier/environment is all held static. What if we throw more compute and scale up the environment too?

title: RARO: Reasoning via Adversarial Inverse RL from Demonstrations
text:
Authors: Locke Cai, Ivan Provilkov
Link: arXiv
Read date: 2026-03-05

title: RARO: Reasoning via Adversarial Inverse RL from Demonstrations
text:
Authors: Locke Cai, Ivan Provilkov
Link: arXiv
Read date: 2026-03-05

title: RARO: Reasoning via Adversarial Inverse RL from Demonstrations
text:
Summary
Most RL-based reasoning training for LLMs requires task-specific verifiers (e.g. checking math answers), but most real-world tasks lack such verifiers. RARO (Relativistic Adversarial Reasoning Optimization) addresses this by using inverse reinforcement learning to train reasoning from expert demonstrations alone. A shared LLM serves as both policy and critic: the policy generates reasoning traces while the critic learns to distinguish expert from policy-generated answers in a relativistic pairwise manner. Joint training via RL with stabilization techniques (replay buffer, tie rewards, loss weighting) yields strong results across verifiable tasks (math, arithmetic) and non-verifiable tasks (poetry), approaching oracle-verifier RL performance on verifiable benchmarks without ever using a verifier.

title: RARO: Reasoning via Adversarial Inverse RL from Demonstrations
text:
Key Ideas

Verifier-free reasoning training — Most tasks lack ground-truth verifiers; RARO removes this dependency by learning rewards from expert demonstrations via IRL.
Relativistic critic — Instead of absolute scoring, the critic compares policy and expert outputs pairwise, determining which is better or declaring a tie. This prevents degeneracy when the policy approaches expert quality.
Shared policy-critic architecture — Both components use the same LLM, reducing memory overhead and enabling “data mixing” where policy and critic rollouts combine in single batches.
Stabilization techniques — Replay buffer (prevents critic forgetting), tie rewards with hyperparameter τ (provides stability near expert quality), and loss weighting (balances objectives).
Emergent behaviors — Self-correcting search in arithmetic; explicit planning in poetry generation—behaviors not present in SFT baselines.

title: RARO: Reasoning via Adversarial Inverse RL from Demonstrations
text:
Method

IRL formulation: Rather than maximizing marginal likelihood of expert CoT traces (intractable), learn a parameterized reward function where policy optimization yields near-optimal performance.
Adversarial game: Policy tries to generate outputs indistinguishable from expert; critic tries to distinguish them. Joint training converges when policy matches expert distribution.
Relativistic comparison: Critic sees (question, answer_A, answer_B) and outputs which is better or tie. This avoids reward collapse when policy quality approaches expert level.
Shared LLM: Single model with different prompts/heads for policy and critic roles. Reduces 2× memory cost to ~1×.
Training loop: Alternate between policy rollouts (optimized with critic rewards via RL) and critic updates (trained to discriminate expert vs. policy). Replay buffer stores past policy outputs for critic stability.

title: RARO: Reasoning via Adversarial Inverse RL from Demonstrations
text:
Results

Countdown (arithmetic): 54.4% accuracy vs. 40.7% SFT, nearly matching RLVR oracle at 57.7%.
DeepMath (103k math problems): 57.5% at 7B vs. 49.3% best baseline; consistent gains across 1.5B–7B scales.
Poetry (40k poems, non-verifiable): 25.0% win-rate vs. expert (vs. 5.9% SFT); 77.3/100 score at 7B.
Scaling: Stable training dynamics with continuous reward/length growth; similar scaling trends to oracle verifier methods.
Baselines beaten: Outperforms SFT, logit-based rewards, and preference optimization (DPO-style) approaches.

title: RARO: Reasoning via Adversarial Inverse RL from Demonstrations
text:
Strengths

Addresses a real bottleneck: most tasks lack verifiers, so verifier-free reasoning training is highly valuable.
Strong empirical results across both verifiable and non-verifiable domains.
Shared architecture is practical—no need for separate reward model.
Relativistic critic design is elegant and well-motivated (avoids reward saturation).
Demonstrates emergent reasoning behaviors (self-correction, planning) beyond what SFT achieves.

title: RARO: Reasoning via Adversarial Inverse RL from Demonstrations
text:
Weaknesses / Limitations

Sample efficiency lags behind RLVR on verifiable tasks—requires more training iterations to match oracle verifier performance.
Training instability observed in long-form creative domains.
Only tested up to 7B parameters; unclear how it scales to larger models.
Requires “abundant expert demonstrations”—data requirements not deeply analyzed.
Critic interpretability is limited; hard to understand what the learned reward captures.
Comparison to recent RLHF/RLAIF methods (which also don’t need task verifiers) could be more thorough.

title: RARO: Reasoning via Adversarial Inverse RL from Demonstrations
text:
Relevance to My Work

GRPO without verifiers — RARO offers an alternative when ground-truth rewards aren’t available. If working on tasks where outcome verification is hard (e.g. clinical reasoning in MedEnv), this could be a practical path.
Self-distillation connection — The adversarial framework is related to self-distillation: the model learns from expert traces but through RL rather than SFT, preserving exploration. Connects to ideas about when distillation vs. RL is more effective.
Reward learning — The relativistic critic could complement GRPO-style training by providing learned rewards for problems where binary correctness isn’t available.
Complements POPE — Where POPE uses oracle solutions as exploration guides (still needs a verifier for rewards), RARO eliminates the verifier entirely but needs more expert demonstrations.

title: RARO: Reasoning via Adversarial Inverse RL from Demonstrations
text:
Key Takeaways

Inverse RL from demonstrations can replace task-specific verifiers for reasoning training, approaching oracle-verifier performance on math while extending to non-verifiable domains.
Relativistic pairwise comparison is more stable than absolute scoring for the critic, especially as policy quality approaches expert level.
Shared policy-critic architecture makes adversarial IRL practical for LLMs without doubling memory costs.
Verifier-free methods are less sample-efficient than RLVR but unlock training on the vast majority of tasks where verifiers don’t exist.
Emergent reasoning behaviors (self-correction, planning) arise from RL training that don’t appear with SFT alone.

title: RLAC: RL with Adversarial Critic for Free-Form Generation
text:
Authors: Mian Wu, Gavin Zhang, Sewon Min, Sergey Levine, Aviral Kumar (UC Berkeley)
Link: arXiv
Read date: 2026-03-06

title: RLAC: RL with Adversarial Critic for Free-Form Generation
text:
Authors: Mian Wu, Gavin Zhang, Sewon Min, Sergey Levine, Aviral Kumar (UC Berkeley)
Link: arXiv
Read date: 2026-03-06

title: RLAC: RL with Adversarial Critic for Free-Form Generation
text:
Summary
Scaling RL post-training to free-form generation (biography writing, code generation) is hard because evaluation requires checking many rubrics/criteria, and exhaustive verification is computationally prohibitive. RLAC frames this as an adversarial game: a critic LLM dynamically proposes the rubric the generator is most likely to fail, and an external validator checks only that rubric. The generator is rewarded when the critic can’t find a failure; the critic is rewarded when it correctly identifies one. Both are trained jointly via DPO with binary validator feedback. This achieves strong results on factual text generation and code generation while using 5–97% fewer verification calls than exhaustive approaches, and avoids reward hacking that plagues static critics.

title: RLAC: RL with Adversarial Critic for Free-Form Generation
text:
Key Ideas

Exhaustive verification doesn’t scale — Free-form tasks have many evaluation criteria; checking all of them per sample is computationally prohibitive (e.g. 7.86M test executions for code).
Static critics get exploited — Fixed reward models or frozen critics are vulnerable to reward hacking as the generator improves. The generator learns to exploit blind spots.
Adversarial min-max game — The critic proposes the worst-case rubric (most likely failure mode); the validator checks it. Generator maximizes performance, critic maximizes failure detection. Both co-evolve.
Prompt-specific, verifiable supervision — Unlike generic reward models, the critic generates task-specific failure hypotheses that are verified against ground truth, maintaining signal fidelity.
Massive efficiency gains — By checking only the most informative rubric per sample, RLAC reduces verification calls by 5.7× (text) to 97.5% (code) compared to exhaustive methods.

title: RLAC: RL with Adversarial Critic for Free-Form Generation
text:
Method

Adversarial formulation: Min-max game where generator maximizes expected reward and critic identifies worst-case rubrics. Reformulated as: generator wins if critic can’t find a failing rubric; critic wins if it correctly pinpoints a failure.
Training: Both generator and critic trained via DPO using binary feedback from external validator. Iterative process: sample outputs → critic proposes criteria → validator checks → update both policies.
External validators: Ground-truth checkers (e.g. fact-checking APIs for biography, test case execution for code). These provide reliable but expensive binary signals.
Critic adaptation: The critic co-evolves with the generator, continuously finding new failure modes as old ones are fixed. This prevents the reward hacking plateau seen with static critics.

title: RLAC: RL with Adversarial Critic for Free-Form Generation
text:
Results

Factual text generation (Biography):

Qwen3-8B: FactScore 0.889, surpassing FactTune-FS (0.867)
5.7× fewer verification calls than exhaustive checking

Code generation:

Qwen2.5-Coder-7B: 56.6 average score across benchmarks
Outperformed AceCoder methods using only 9% of training data
97.5% fewer test case executions (192k vs. 7.86M)

Ablations:

Noisy validation destabilizes training below baseline
Static critics plateau; adversarial critics maintain detection effectiveness
Dynamic adaptation is key to preventing reward hacking

title: RLAC: RL with Adversarial Critic for Free-Form Generation
text:
Strengths

Elegant adversarial formulation that naturally focuses compute on the most informative evaluation criteria.
Massive efficiency gains make RL practical for free-form tasks where exhaustive verification was infeasible.
Co-evolving critic prevents reward hacking—a real and common failure mode with static reward models.
Demonstrated across qualitatively different domains (factual text, code) with different verification structures.
Clean experimental design with informative ablations (noisy validator, static vs. adversarial critic).

title: RLAC: RL with Adversarial Critic for Free-Form Generation
text:
Weaknesses / Limitations

Requires an external validator for each rubric—still needs ground-truth checking infrastructure, just less of it.
DPO-based training for both generator and critic; unclear how it interacts with online RL methods like GRPO.
Evaluated only on 7–8B models; scaling behavior to larger models unknown.
The critic must be expressive enough to generate meaningful failure hypotheses; may struggle on tasks with subtle or hard-to-articulate failure modes.
Adversarial dynamics can be unstable; paper reports stabilization but long-term training dynamics not deeply analyzed.

title: RLAC: RL with Adversarial Critic for Free-Form Generation
text:
Relevance to My Work

Self-distilling reward models connection — RLAC’s adversarial critic is conceptually related to the self-judge idea: both use an LLM to generate evaluation signals. The key difference is RLAC uses a separate critic model + external validator, while self-distilling reward models use the same model with asymmetric information. Could combine: use the policy itself as the adversarial critic, grounded by environment feedback.
Reward hacking mitigation — The adversarial critic that co-evolves with the generator directly addresses reward hacking. Relevant to the reward hack detection angle of the self-distilling reward models idea.
Efficiency for RLVR — The “check only the most informative criterion” principle could apply to RLVR settings with multiple verification signals (e.g. multiple test cases in code).
Dense rewards from sparse verification — Similar motivation to the self-judge work: turn expensive/sparse verification into more informative training signals.

title: RLAC: RL with Adversarial Critic for Free-Form Generation
text:
Key Takeaways

Free-form generation RL can be made practical by adversarially selecting which rubrics to verify, rather than checking all of them.
Co-evolving the critic with the generator is essential—static critics get exploited and lead to reward hacking plateaus.
The adversarial framework achieves 5–97% verification cost reduction while matching or exceeding exhaustive verification quality.
External ground-truth validators remain necessary but are used far more efficiently as targeted checks on critic-proposed failure modes.
Same research group (Aviral Kumar) as POPE—both address making RL post-training more practical, from different angles (exploration vs. evaluation).

title: DeepSeekMath: Pushing the Limits of Mathematical Reasoning (GRPO)
text:
Summary
Introduces GRPO (Group Relative Policy Optimization) as a variant of PPO that avoids learning a separate value function. Instead of a critic, it estimates baselines from a group of sampled outputs for the same prompt. This dramatically simplifies the RL pipeline.

title: DeepSeekMath: Pushing the Limits of Mathematical Reasoning (GRPO)
text:
Key Ideas

No critic needed — sample \(G\) completions per prompt, use the group mean reward as baseline. This removes an entire model from the training loop.
RLVR (RL with Verifiable Rewards) — works best when you have binary/verifiable correctness signals (math proofs, code execution). Directly applicable to our clinical task verification setup.
Outcome supervision vs process supervision — they show outcome-level rewards work surprisingly well when paired with GRPO, even without step-level annotations.

title: DeepSeekMath: Pushing the Limits of Mathematical Reasoning (GRPO)
text:
Relevance to MedEnv

Our FHIR action verification gives us exactly the kind of verifiable reward GRPO is designed for
The “no critic” property is huge for us — training a value function over clinical trajectories would be extremely expensive
Group sampling maps well to generating multiple clinical plans and rewarding the best ones

title: DeepSeekMath: Pushing the Limits of Mathematical Reasoning (GRPO)
text:
Critiques / Limitations

Group size \(G\) is a key hyperparameter they don’t ablate thoroughly — matters a lot for sample efficiency
Unclear how well this extends to very long trajectories (their math tasks are relatively short)
The verifiable reward assumption is strong; many clinical tasks have ambiguous correctness

title: DeepSeekMath: Pushing the Limits of Mathematical Reasoning (GRPO)
text:
Quotes to Remember

(paraphrase) GRPO replaces the value model baseline with the group mean, reducing memory and compute by ~50% compared to PPO.

title: Designing your own RL engine
text: In this post, we’ll guide you step by step through designing your own asynchronous, distributed reinforcement learning (RL) engine purpose-built for post-training large language models (LLMs). Techniques like RLHF (Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback), RLVR, and RLFT (Reinforcement Learning from various feedback signals) have become central to advancing LLM capabilities, yet often seem like black magic. Here, we’ll peel back the curtain and construct a principled, modular RL post-training system from the ground up.

title: Designing your own RL engine
text:
Why pursue this?

Gain a deep understanding of the core components that power modern RL pipelines for LLMs
Develop the flexibility to customize, extend, and debug RL-based systems to fit your unique needs and experiments
Demystify complex RL post-training techniques such as RLHF, RLVR, and RLFT by building them piece by piece
Empower your team with a reproducible, modular framework that can scale from prototypes to production

title: Designing your own RL engine
text:
Setting the grounds: standard GRPO
In raw PyTorch, you’d do something like this:
import torch
from transformers import AutoModelForCausalLM, AutoTokenizer

# 1. Load model
model = AutoModelForCausalLM.from_pretrained("Qwen/Qwen3-4B")
ref_model = AutoModelForCausalLM.from_pretrained("Qwen/Qwen3-4B")  # frozen copy
tokenizer = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained("Qwen/Qwen3-4B")
optimizer = torch.optim.Adam(model.parameters(), lr=1e-6)

# 2. Reward function
def reward_fn(completion, ground_truth):
    answer = extract_answer(completion)
    return 1.0 if answer == ground_truth else 0.0

# 3. Training loop
for batch in dataloader:
    prompts = batch["prompt"]
    truths = batch["ground_truth"]

    # === GENERATE ROLLOUTS ===
    G = 8  # rollouts per prompt
    all_completions = []
    all_old_logprobs = []

    model.eval()
    with torch.no_grad():
        for prompt in prompts:
            input_ids = tokenizer(prompt, return_tensors="pt").input_ids
            for _ in range(G):
                # sample a completion
                output = model.generate(input_ids, temperature=1.0, max_new_tokens=1024)
                completion_ids = output[:, input_ids.shape[1]:]

                # record log-probs under current (old) policy
                logits = model(output).logits[:, input_ids.shape[1]-1:-1, :]
                logprobs = torch.log_softmax(logits, dim=-1)
                token_logprobs = logprobs.gather(2, completion_ids.unsqueeze(2)).squeeze(2)

                all_completions.append(completion_ids)
                all_old_logprobs.append(token_logprobs)

    # === COMPUTE REWARDS AND ADVANTAGES ===
    rewards = []
    for i, prompt in enumerate(prompts):
        for g in range(G):
            text = tokenizer.decode(all_completions[i * G + g][0])
            rewards.append(reward_fn(text, truths[i]))

    # group advantages: A_i = r_i - mean(group)
    advantages = []
    for i in range(len(prompts)):
        group_rewards = rewards[i * G : (i + 1) * G]
        mean_r = sum(group_rewards) / G
        for r in group_rewards:
            advantages.append(r - mean_r)

    # === POLICY GRADIENT UPDATE ===
    model.train()
    epsilon = 0.2
    total_loss = 0

    for idx in range(len(all_completions)):
        completion_ids = all_completions[idx]
        old_logprobs = all_old_logprobs[idx]
        A = advantages[idx]

        # forward pass through CURRENT model
        logits = model(completion_ids).logits[:, :-1, :]
        logprobs = torch.log_softmax(logits, dim=-1)
        new_logprobs = logprobs.gather(2, completion_ids[:, 1:].unsqueeze(2)).squeeze(2)

        # ratio
        ratio = torch.exp(new_logprobs - old_logprobs)

        # clipped loss
        unclipped = ratio * A
        clipped = torch.clamp(ratio, 1 - epsilon, 1 + epsilon) * A
        loss = -torch.min(unclipped, clipped).mean()

        total_loss += loss

    # standard PyTorch update — the familiar part
    optimizer.zero_grad()
    total_loss.backward()
    optimizer.step()
but… this is a problem because:

slow, will it vram issues
does not separate training and sampling.
In sampling, we want TP, but in training we want FSDP.
We can do this on two nodes separately and not have to worry but what if you only have one node?
Thus, we design a system that takes the above code and extends it to doing async, dual training-generation
we will build an api from the ground up that wraps the above code and produces the desired logic
we extend it all the way to async rl in the form of pipelinerl.
this ends up being a drop in replacement for the likes of verl.
we use vLLM (inference) and PyTorch DDP (training) in the backend to make everything work.

That’s literally what verl is. vLLM for generation (fast, returns log-probs for free), then switches to PyTorch/FSDP for the training step. You’ve just reinvented the motivation for verl from first principles.
You could wire this up yourself — use vLLM as a separate inference server, collect rollouts + log-probs, then feed them into a PyTorch training loop. But you’d have to handle:

Loading/unloading weights between vLLM and PyTorch
Keeping the weights in sync after each optimizer step
Managing GPU memory between the two modes
Sharding for models that don’t fit on one GPU

That’s all the plumbing verl handles for you. The actual GRPO math inside verl’s training step is the same PyTorch code you already understand.
But guess what… we are going to do it anyway to (1) learn!; (2) offer flexibility in the underlying abstractions.

Quick primer

title: Designing your own RL engine
text:
Extending GRPO to async RL
so how can we take the naive GRPO loop above and modify it to satisfy the desired points we set above? Perhaps something like this:
import asyncio
import uuid
import torch
import torch.distributed as dist
from torch.nn.parallel import DistributedDataParallel as DDP
from torch.nn.functional import log_softmax
from transformers import AutoModelForCausalLM, AutoTokenizer
from vllm import AsyncLLMEngine, SamplingParams
from vllm.engine.arg_utils import AsyncEngineArgs
import os

# ─────────────────────────────────────────────
# CONFIG
# ─────────────────────────────────────────────
MODEL_NAME = "Qwen/Qwen3-4B"
G = 8               # rollouts per prompt
B = 4               # prompts per training batch
EPSILON = 0.2
LR = 1e-6
SYNC_EVERY = 1      # weight sync frequency (gradient steps)
MAX_QUEUE = 32      # bounded rollout buffer

# ─────────────────────────────────────────────
# REWARD
# ─────────────────────────────────────────────
def extract_answer(text: str) -> str:
    # placeholder — replace with your actual parser
    import re
    match = re.search(r"#### (.+)", text)
    return match.group(1).strip() if match else ""

def reward_fn(completion: str, ground_truth: str) -> float:
    return 1.0 if extract_answer(completion) == ground_truth else 0.0

# ─────────────────────────────────────────────
# ADVANTAGES
# ─────────────────────────────────────────────
def compute_group_advantages(rewards: list[float], g: int) -> list[float]:
    advantages = []
    n_prompts = len(rewards) // g
    for i in range(n_prompts):
        group = rewards[i * g : (i + 1) * g]
        mean_r = sum(group) / g
        std_r = (sum((r - mean_r) ** 2 for r in group) / g) ** 0.5 + 1e-8
        for r in group:
            advantages.append((r - mean_r) / std_r)
    return advantages

# ─────────────────────────────────────────────
# WEIGHT SYNC: trainer → vLLM
# ─────────────────────────────────────────────
async def sync_weights_to_vllm(model: DDP, vllm_engine: AsyncLLMEngine):
    state_dict = {k: v.cpu() for k, v in model.module.state_dict().items()}
    # vLLM 0.4+ exposes update_weights on the executor
    await asyncio.get_event_loop().run_in_executor(
        None,
        vllm_engine.engine.model_executor.update_weights,
        state_dict,
    )

# ─────────────────────────────────────────────
# TRAINING STEP
# ─────────────────────────────────────────────
def grpo_update(
    model: DDP,
    optimizer: torch.optim.Optimizer,
    tokenizer,
    buffer: list,
    step: int,
) -> float:
    """
    buffer items: (prompt_str, completion_str, old_logprobs: Tensor [L], reward: float)
    old_logprobs come from vLLM per-token logprobs (see SamplingParams logprobs=1).
    """
    prompts, completions, old_logprobs_list, rewards = zip(*buffer)
    advantages = compute_group_advantages(list(rewards), G)

    model.train()
    total_loss = torch.tensor(0.0, device=model.device)

    for completion, old_lp, A in zip(completions, old_logprobs_list, advantages):
        if abs(A) < 1e-8:          # skip zero-advantage tokens (common with sparse rewards)
            continue

        ids = tokenizer(
            completion, return_tensors="pt", truncation=True, max_length=1024
        ).input_ids.to(model.device)

        if ids.shape[1] < 2:
            continue

        logits = model(ids).logits[:, :-1, :]                          # [1, L-1, V]
        new_lp = log_softmax(logits, dim=-1)                           # [1, L-1, V]
        new_token_lp = new_lp.gather(2, ids[:, 1:].unsqueeze(2)).squeeze(2)  # [1, L-1]

        # old_lp: Tensor [L-1] on CPU — move to device
        old_token_lp = old_lp.to(model.device)

        # clamp length mismatch (vLLM may truncate differently)
        min_len = min(new_token_lp.shape[1], old_token_lp.shape[0])
        new_token_lp = new_token_lp[:, :min_len]
        old_token_lp = old_token_lp[:min_len]

        ratio = torch.exp(new_token_lp - old_token_lp)                # [1, L-1]

        unclipped = ratio * A
        clipped   = torch.clamp(ratio, 1 - EPSILON, 1 + EPSILON) * A
        loss = -torch.min(unclipped, clipped).mean()

        total_loss = total_loss + loss

    total_loss = total_loss / len(completions)
    optimizer.zero_grad()
    total_loss.backward()
    torch.nn.utils.clip_grad_norm_(model.parameters(), 1.0)
    optimizer.step()

    return total_loss.item()

# ─────────────────────────────────────────────
# GENERATION WORKER  (runs as async task)
# ─────────────────────────────────────────────
async def generation_worker(
    vllm_engine: AsyncLLMEngine,
    dataloader,
    rollout_queue: asyncio.Queue,
    sampling_params: SamplingParams,
    done_event: asyncio.Event,
):
    for batch in dataloader:
        prompts = batch["prompt"]
        truths  = batch["ground_truth"]

        tasks = []
        for prompt, truth in zip(prompts, truths):
            for _ in range(G):
                tasks.append((prompt, truth))

        # fire all G*B requests concurrently
        async def collect_one(prompt, truth):
            request_id = uuid.uuid4().hex
            async for output in vllm_engine.generate(prompt, sampling_params, request_id):
                if output.finished:
                    out = output.outputs[0]
                    completion = out.text

                    # per-token logprobs from vLLM: list[dict[token_id -> logprob]]
                    if out.logprobs:
                        # take the top-1 logprob (the sampled token) at each position
                        token_lps = torch.tensor(
                            [list(step.values())[0].logprob for step in out.logprobs],
                            dtype=torch.float32,
                        )
                    else:
                        # fallback: use cumulative_logprob broadcast (less accurate)
                        n = max(len(completion.split()), 1)
                        token_lps = torch.full((n,), out.cumulative_logprob / n)

                    r = reward_fn(completion, truth)
                    await rollout_queue.put((prompt, completion, token_lps, r))

        await asyncio.gather(*[collect_one(p, t) for p, t in tasks])

    done_event.set()

# ─────────────────────────────────────────────
# TRAINING WORKER  (runs as async task)
# ─────────────────────────────────────────────
async def training_worker(
    model: DDP,
    optimizer: torch.optim.Optimizer,
    tokenizer,
    vllm_engine: AsyncLLMEngine,
    rollout_queue: asyncio.Queue,
    done_event: asyncio.Event,
):
    buffer = []
    step   = 0
    batch_size = B * G

    while True:
        # drain queue or exit when generation is done and queue is empty
        try:
            item = await asyncio.wait_for(rollout_queue.get(), timeout=5.0)
            buffer.append(item)
            rollout_queue.task_done()
        except asyncio.TimeoutError:
            if done_event.is_set() and rollout_queue.empty():
                break
            continue

        if len(buffer) >= batch_size:
            loss = grpo_update(model, optimizer, tokenizer, buffer[:batch_size], step)
            buffer = buffer[batch_size:]
            step += 1

            print(f"[step {step}] loss={loss:.4f}")

            if step % SYNC_EVERY == 0:
                await sync_weights_to_vllm(model, vllm_engine)
                print(f"[step {step}] weights synced to vLLM")

# ─────────────────────────────────────────────
# MAIN
# ─────────────────────────────────────────────
async def main(dataloader):
    # ── inference engine: GPUs 0-1, tensor parallel ──
    engine_args = AsyncEngineArgs(
        model=MODEL_NAME,
        tensor_parallel_size=2,
        gpu_memory_utilization=0.45,
        dtype="bfloat16",
    )
    vllm_engine = AsyncLLMEngine.from_engine_args(engine_args)

    sampling_params = SamplingParams(
        temperature=1.0,
        max_tokens=1024,
        logprobs=1,        # get per-token logprobs from vLLM
    )

    # ── training model: GPUs 2-3, DDP ──
    os.environ["MASTER_ADDR"] = "localhost"
    os.environ["MASTER_PORT"] = "29500"
    dist.init_process_group(backend="nccl", world_size=2, rank=0)

    tokenizer = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained(MODEL_NAME)
    model = AutoModelForCausalLM.from_pretrained(MODEL_NAME, torch_dtype=torch.bfloat16)
    model = model.to("cuda:2")
    model = DDP(model, device_ids=[2, 3])

    optimizer = torch.optim.AdamW(model.parameters(), lr=LR, weight_decay=0.01)

    # ── shared rollout buffer ──
    rollout_queue = asyncio.Queue(maxsize=MAX_QUEUE)
    done_event    = asyncio.Event()

    # ── run generation and training concurrently ──
    await asyncio.gather(
        generation_worker(vllm_engine, dataloader, rollout_queue, sampling_params, done_event),
        training_worker(model, optimizer, tokenizer, vllm_engine, rollout_queue, done_event),
    )

    dist.destroy_process_group()


if __name__ == "__main__":
    # placeholder dataloader — swap with your actual one
    class FakeDataloader:
        def __iter__(self):
            for _ in range(100):
                yield {
                    "prompt": ["Solve: 2 + 2"] * B,
                    "ground_truth": ["4"] * B,
                }

    asyncio.run(main(FakeDataloader()))
Annotated…
Here’s the naive loop modified in place, step by step, to get async dual training-generation on a single node:
Step 1: Separate generation onto vLLM
Replace the model.generate(...) block with a vLLM AsyncLLMEngine call. vLLM handles tensor parallelism internally, so you spin it up on e.g. GPUs 0-1 with tensor_parallel_size=2.
from vllm import AsyncLLMEngine, SamplingParams
from vllm.engine.arg_utils import AsyncEngineArgs

engine_args = AsyncEngineArgs(
    model="Qwen/Qwen3-4B",
    tensor_parallel_size=2,   # GPUs 0-1
    gpu_memory_utilization=0.45,
)
vllm_engine = AsyncLLMEngine.from_engine_args(engine_args)
sampling_params = SamplingParams(temperature=1.0, max_tokens=1024)
The training model runs on GPUs 2-3 with DDP:
import torch.distributed as dist
# training process group: ranks 2-3
model = DDP(model.to("cuda:2"))
Step 2: Decouple generation from training with a shared queue
Instead of generate → compute rewards → update (sequential), use a asyncio.Queue (or multiprocessing.Queue across processes):
import asyncio

rollout_queue = asyncio.Queue(maxsize=32)  # bounded to avoid staleness

async def generation_worker():
    for batch in dataloader:
        for prompt in batch["prompts"]:
            for _ in range(G):
                request_id = uuid.uuid4().hex
                async for output in vllm_engine.generate(prompt, sampling_params, request_id):
                    if output.finished:
                        completion = output.outputs[0].text
                        # get old logprobs from vLLM output (cumulative logprob)
                        old_logprob = output.outputs[0].cumulative_logprob
                        await rollout_queue.put((prompt, completion, old_logprob, batch["ground_truth"]))

async def training_worker():
    buffer = []
    async for item in drain(rollout_queue, batch_size=B * G):
        buffer.append(item)
        if len(buffer) >= B * G:
            grpo_update(model, optimizer, buffer)
            buffer = []
            await sync_weights_to_vllm()   # see step 3
Step 3: Weight sync
After every gradient step (or every K steps), serialize the trained weights and hot-reload into vLLM:
async def sync_weights_to_vllm():
    state_dict = {k: v.cpu() for k, v in model.module.state_dict().items()}
    # vLLM 0.4+ supports update_weights via its llm_engine
    await vllm_engine.engine.model_executor.update_weights(state_dict)
Step 4: Token-level old logprobs + IS ratio
vLLM’s cumulative_logprob is sequence-level. For per-token logprobs (needed for the PPO clip), either: - Re-run the training model on the completion to get old_logprobs right before the update (adds a forward pass but keeps it simple), or - Enable logprobs=True in SamplingParams to get per-token logprobs from vLLM directly.
sampling_params = SamplingParams(temperature=1.0, max_tokens=1024, logprobs=1)
# output.outputs[0].logprobs is a list of {token_id: logprob} dicts
The modified inner loop (training side) stays almost identical to the original, just consuming from the queue:
def grpo_update(model, optimizer, buffer):
    prompts, completions, old_logprobs_list, truths = zip(*buffer)
    
    # same advantage computation as before
    rewards = [reward_fn(c, t) for c, t in zip(completions, truths)]
    advantages = compute_group_advantages(rewards, G)

    model.train()
    total_loss = 0
    for completion, old_lp, A in zip(completions, old_logprobs_list, advantages):
        ids = tokenizer(completion, return_tensors="pt").input_ids.to(model.device)
        logits = model(ids).logits[:, :-1, :]
        new_lp = log_softmax(logits).gather(2, ids[:, 1:].unsqueeze(2)).squeeze(2)
        
        ratio = torch.exp(new_lp - old_lp)
        loss = -torch.min(ratio * A, torch.clamp(ratio, 1-ε, 1+ε) * A).mean()
        total_loss += loss

    optimizer.zero_grad()
    total_loss.backward()
    optimizer.step()

The structure is now:
[vLLM: GPUs 0-1] --rollouts--> [Queue] --batches--> [DDP: GPUs 2-3]
                                                          |
                                  <---weights synced------+
Generation and training overlap in time. This is exactly what verl does under the hood — once this works, wrapping it into a clean RLEngine API is straightforward.

title: Designing your own RL engine
text:
API design
Great. we don’t want to have to write out all that complex distributed and async logic everytime! So let’s design a little api we can use repeatedly. How might we do this?
Perhaps something like this?:
from minirl import RLEngine

engine = RLEngine(
    model_path="Qwen/Qwen3-4B",
    lr=1e-6,
    tp_size=1,          # tensor parallel for vLLM
    fsdp=True,          # use FSDP for training
)

for step in range(num_steps):
    # vLLM mode — fast generation with log-probs
    rollouts = engine.generate(prompts, n=8, temperature=1.0, max_tokens=1024)

    # switch GPU memory layout
    engine.switch_to_train()

    # familiar PyTorch — engine.model is an FSDP-wrapped model
    for epoch in range(4):
        for mb in rollouts.iter_minibatches(size=8):
            new_lp = engine.get_logprobs(mb.token_ids)
            ratio = torch.exp(new_lp - mb.old_logprobs)
            # ... compute clipped loss ...
            engine.step(loss)

    engine.switch_to_generate()

Altnerative designs
What about other designs? the design space is quite large…
Here are a few alternative designs:
Design A (what we have): Explicit mode switching
engine = RLEngine(model_path="Qwen/Qwen3-4B")
rollouts = engine.generate(prompts, n=8)
engine.switch_to_train()
# ... manual training loop ...
engine.switch_to_generate()
Design B: Context managers
engine = RLEngine(model_path="Qwen/Qwen3-4B")

with engine.generation_mode() as generator:
    rollouts = generator(prompts, n=8)

with engine.training_mode() as (model, optimizer):
    logits = model(token_ids)
    loss.backward()
    optimizer.step()
# switching happens automatically on __enter__ / __exit__
Design C: Callback-based
engine = RLEngine(model_path="Qwen/Qwen3-4B")

def my_reward_fn(completions, truths):
    return [1.0 if correct else 0.0 for ...]

def my_train_step(model, optimizer, rollouts):
    # full control over loss computation
    ...

engine.train(
    prompts=prompts,
    reward_fn=my_reward_fn,
    train_fn=my_train_step,
    n=8, num_epochs=4,
)
Design D: Minimal — just a smart weight manager
wm = WeightManager(model_path="Qwen/Qwen3-4B")

llm = wm.as_vllm(tp_size=1)           # returns vLLM LLM object
rollouts = llm.generate(...)

model, optimizer = wm.as_trainable()    # returns FSDP model + optimizer (weights synced)
loss.backward()
optimizer.step()

llm = wm.as_vllm()                     # updated weights, optimizer state preserved
Design D is the thinnest wrapper — it only solves the weight transfer problem and lets you use raw vLLM and raw PyTorch directly.


Which one?
But how should we choose a design? Are there certain design principles we should follow? Glad you asked! Think about the following things:
Not subjective — there are real principles. The most relevant ones here:

Separation of concerns. Each component should do one thing. Design D is strongest here — the weight manager only manages weights. It doesn’t know about GRPO or rewards. Design C is weakest — engine.train() mixes orchestration, reward, and training into one call.
Inversion of control. Who’s in charge? In Designs A/B/D, your code controls the loop — you decide when to generate, when to train, how many epochs. In Design C, the library controls the loop and calls your functions. The principle: if the user needs flexibility (which you do for GLARE — custom losses, auxiliary heads), inversion of control should favor the user. So A/B/D are better for your case.
Principle of least surprise. The API should behave like things the user already knows. Design D wins — wm.as_vllm() returns a real vLLM.LLM object, wm.as_trainable() returns a real nn.Module + optimizer. You already know how to use both. No new abstractions to learn.
Leaky abstraction principle. Abstractions that hide complexity will eventually leak. Design A’s engine.get_logprobs() hides the forward pass — but what if you need logits too, for your reward head? Now you need to break the abstraction. Design D doesn’t have this problem because it gives you the raw objects.
Composability. Can you combine this with other tools? Design D composes cleanly — it’s just a weight manager, so you can add wandb logging, custom schedulers, gradient clipping, anything. Design C is hardest to compose because the library owns the loop.


All four designs face the same underlying hardware problem: the same GPUs need to switch between vLLM’s tensor-parallel layout and FSDP’s sharded layout. No API design changes the physics of GPU memory. The problems are in the implementation layer, not the interface layer. Whether you call it engine.switch_to_train(), with engine.training_mode(), or wm.as_trainable(), underneath it still needs to reshard weights and move optimizer states.

title: Designing your own RL engine
text:
Note: colocation vs. async rl
Two paradigms:
Co-located (verl): Same GPUs do both jobs, taking turns. Generate → train → generate → train. GPUs switch modes via resharding. The same GPUs need to switch between vLLM’s tensor-parallel layout and FSDP’s sharded layout.
Async/decoupled: Separate GPU pools. Pool A generates nonstop, Pool B trains nonstop. They communicate via a rollout queue. No resharding needed — each pool stays in its mode permanently.
One nuance: “async” doesn’t mean the pools are equal halves. You can allocate, say, 70% to generation and 30% to training, since generation is usually the bottleneck. The split is tunable.



# === Co-located (what we designed) ===
from minirl import WeightManager

wm = WeightManager(model_path="Qwen/Qwen3-4B")
llm = wm.as_vllm()
rollouts = llm.generate(...)
model, optimizer = wm.as_trainable()
# ... train ...


# === Async (separate GPU pools) ===
from minirl import RolloutServer, TrainWorker

# Launch on GPU pool A (inference machines)
server = RolloutServer(
    model_path="Qwen/Qwen3-4B",
    tp_size=2,
    queue_address="tcp://trainer:5555",
)
server.run()  # generates nonstop, pushes rollouts to queue

# Launch on GPU pool B (training machines)
worker = TrainWorker(
    model_path="Qwen/Qwen3-4B",
    queue_address="tcp://trainer:5555",
)
for rollouts in worker.consume():       # pulls from queue
    model, optimizer = worker.model, worker.optimizer
    # ... same training loop ...
    worker.broadcast_weights()           # pushes updated weights to server

title: Research Interests
text: My research sits at the intersection of reinforcement learning, large language models, and robot learning. Below is a more detailed account of the specific directions I am pursuing and the questions that drive my work.

title: Research Interests
text:
RL Post-Training for Language Models
A central theme of my research is understanding how reinforcement learning can be used after supervised pre-training to unlock new capabilities in large language models. Recent work has shown that RL-based post-training—such as RLHF [1] and RLVR [2]—can substantially improve reasoning, instruction following, and alignment. I am particularly interested in:

Self-distillation and self-play: can models improve by training on their own outputs, bootstrapping capability without new human data? This connects to ideas from expert iteration [3] and self-play in games [4].
Reward modeling and reward hacking: how do we design reward signals that remain faithful proxies for human intent as models become more capable? The problem of reward hacking [5] is a key bottleneck.
Continual RL post-training: rather than a single RL fine-tuning phase, can we develop curricula that iteratively refine models over time, akin to continual learning [6]?

title: Research Interests
text:
Robot Learning and Visuomotor Policies
The second pillar of my work concerns learning sensorimotor policies for robotic manipulation. Foundation models pre-trained on internet-scale data provide rich representations, but bridging the gap to physical action remains an open challenge. I am exploring:

Vision-language-action models (VLAs): architectures that map visual observations and language instructions directly to robot actions [7], and how to fine-tune them efficiently for new tasks.
On-policy RL for robotics: methods like PPO [8] and GRPO [2] applied in simulation and on real hardware, with a focus on sample efficiency and sim-to-real transfer.
World models and planning: learning predictive models of environment dynamics [9] that enable model-based planning and reduce the need for costly real-world interaction.

title: Research Interests
text:
Unifying Theme
RL is the connective tissue across these interests. Whether the “backbone” is a language model or a visuomotor policy, the core algorithmic questions are shared: how to define good reward signals, how to explore efficiently, and how to leverage pre-trained representations for downstream adaptation. I believe progress on these fronts will be mutually reinforcing—advances in RL post-training for LLMs will inform robot learning, and vice versa.

title: On-Policy Self-Distillation for Cross-Embodiment Transfer in World Action Models
text:
World Action Models (WAMs) such as DreamZero jointly predict future video and robot actions through a shared backbone, achieving strong generalization on manipulation tasks. A key promise of WAMs is cross-embodiment transfer: leveraging abundant video demonstrations from other robots or humans to improve a target robot’s policy. However, current approaches use cross-embodiment data in a limited way—training only a video prediction objective on non-target data, discarding the action head entirely. The model learns what the world looks like when a task is performed but never explicitly learns how the target robot should act given what another embodiment demonstrated.
This is a surprisingly impoverished learning signal. The shared video-action backbone already encodes correspondences between visual dynamics and motor commands for the target embodiment. Cross-embodiment demonstrations provide strong constraints on the desired visual trajectory, grasp strategy, and timing. But no training signal connects these two sources of knowledge for action prediction.
We propose OPSD-CE (On-Policy Self-Distillation for Cross-Embodiment transfer), a method that uses cross-embodiment demonstrations as privileged information to generate dense, per-timestep action supervision for the target embodiment—then distills this knowledge into a student that requires no demonstrations at inference time.

title: On-Policy Self-Distillation for Cross-Embodiment Transfer in World Action Models
