title: On-Policy Self-Distillation for Cross-Embodiment Transfer in World Action Models
text:
2. Background

2.1 DreamZero and World Action Models
DreamZero is a 14B-parameter WAM built on a Diffusion Transformer (DiT) backbone that jointly predicts video tokens and action tokens via flow matching. Given a task instruction \(x\) and current observation \(s_0\), the model autoregressively generates future video frames \(\hat{v}_{1:T}\) and extracts actions \(a_{1:T}\) through an implicit inverse dynamics process. The video prediction acts as a visual planner: the model first “imagines” the future, then derives motor commands consistent with that imagined future.
For cross-embodiment data (e.g., human demonstrations, other robot platforms), DreamZero trains only the video prediction loss:
\[\mathcal{L}_{\text{cross}} = \mathcal{L}_{\text{video}}(\theta;\, x,\, v^{\text{demo}})\]
discarding the action loss since action labels are unavailable or incompatible. This yields a 42% improvement with 10–20 minutes of cross-embodiment video, but leaves substantial information unused.
Key observation: DreamZero’s authors identify that the primary failure mode is video generation errors propagating to incorrect actions—not action extraction failures. The action head is capable when given good visual plans, but the visual planner sometimes errs.


2.2 On-Policy Self-Distillation (OPSD)
OPSD introduces a framework for distilling privileged information into a student policy through on-policy rollouts. A teacher \(\pi_\theta^T(y \mid x, I)\) conditions on privileged information \(I\) unavailable at test time, while a student \(\pi_\phi^S(y \mid x)\) sees only the input \(x\). The training procedure:

The student generates rollouts from its own policy.
At each token position along the rollout, the teacher provides a target distribution conditioned on \(I\).
The student minimizes per-token divergence against the teacher’s distribution.

OPSD achieves 4–8× token efficiency over GRPO by replacing sparse end-of-sequence rewards with dense per-token supervision. The on-policy rollouts avoid distribution mismatch between training and inference.

title: On-Policy Self-Distillation for Cross-Embodiment Transfer in World Action Models
text:
3. Method: OPSD-CE

3.1 Setup
Let \(\pi_\theta\) denote the shared WAM with parameters \(\theta\). We define two operating modes of the same model, controlled by a gating scalar \(\alpha \in [0, 1]\):
\[\textbf{Teacher:} \quad \pi_\theta^T(a_t \mid s_t, x, v^{\text{demo}};\; \alpha = 1)\]
\[\textbf{Student:} \quad \pi_\theta^S(a_t \mid s_t, x;\; \alpha = 0)\]
where \(v^{\text{demo}}\) is a cross-embodiment demonstration video (privileged information), \(s_t\) is the state at timestep \(t\), and \(x\) is the task instruction.
No additional parameters. The cross-embodiment demonstration \(v^{\text{demo}}\) is tokenized by the existing video tokenizer and injected into the DiT backbone via cross-attention, gated by \(\alpha\). When \(\alpha = 0\), the demonstration tokens are zeroed out and the model operates as the student. When \(\alpha = 1\), the model has full access (teacher mode). Both modes share all parameters \(\theta\).


3.2 Why the Teacher Can Rationalize
The teacher is not an oracle—it must infer appropriate target-embodiment actions from a cross-embodiment demonstration. We argue this is tractable because:

Shared visual dynamics. The demonstration constrains the desired visual trajectory (object motion, contact timing, spatial path). These are largely embodiment-invariant.
Known target-embodiment mapping. From target-embodiment data \(\mathcal{D}_{\text{tgt}}\), the model has learned the mapping from visual trajectories to motor commands for the target robot.
Narrowed search space. Instead of searching over all motor programs that achieve the goal, the teacher only needs to find commands that reproduce the demonstrated visual trajectory on the target embodiment.

The demonstration acts as a strong prior that collapses the ambiguity inherent in instruction-only conditioning.


3.3 Training Algorithm

Algorithm: OPSD-CE
Input: Shared WAM \(\pi_\theta\), target data \(\mathcal{D}_{\text{tgt}}\), cross-embodiment data \(\mathcal{D}_{\text{cross}}\), KL coefficient \(\beta\), learning rate \(\eta\)
Initialize: \(\theta\) from pre-trained WAM
Repeat until convergence:

Standard WAM training on target-embodiment data:

Sample \((x, v_{\text{tgt}}, a_{\text{tgt}}) \sim \mathcal{D}_{\text{tgt}}\)
\(\mathcal{L}_{\text{video}} \leftarrow \text{FlowMatch}(\pi_\theta, x, v_{\text{tgt}})\)
\(\mathcal{L}_{\text{action}} \leftarrow \text{FlowMatch}(\pi_\theta, x, a_{\text{tgt}})\)

Cross-embodiment self-distillation:

Sample \((x, v^{\text{demo}}) \sim \mathcal{D}_{\text{cross}}\)

Phase 1 — Student rollout (on-policy):

Roll out \(\tau^S = (s_0, a_0^S, s_1, a_1^S, \ldots, s_T)\) using \(\pi_\theta^S(\cdot \mid s_t, x;\; \alpha = 0)\)

Phase 2 — Dense teacher supervision:

For each timestep \(t\) in \(\tau^S\):

Sample flow timestep \(t_{\text{flow}} \sim \mathcal{U}[0,1]\), noise \(\epsilon \sim \mathcal{N}(0, I)\)
Teacher velocity: \(\mathbf{v}^T_t \leftarrow f_\theta(s_t, x, v^{\text{demo}}, \epsilon, t_{\text{flow}};\; \alpha = 1)\)
Student velocity: \(\mathbf{v}^S_t \leftarrow f_\theta(s_t, x, \epsilon, t_{\text{flow}};\; \alpha = 0)\)


Phase 3 — Distillation loss:

\(\mathcal{L}_{\text{distill}} \leftarrow \frac{1}{T} \sum_{t=0}^{T-1} \left\| \mathbf{v}^S_t - \text{sg}(\mathbf{v}^T_t) \right\|^2\)

Joint update:

\(\mathcal{L} \leftarrow \mathcal{L}_{\text{video}} + \mathcal{L}_{\text{action}} + \beta \cdot \mathcal{L}_{\text{distill}}\)
\(\theta \leftarrow \theta - \eta \nabla_\theta \mathcal{L}\)


Return: \(\pi_\theta^S\) (student mode, \(\alpha = 0\))



3.4 Design Decisions
Flow-matching MSE instead of KL. Since DreamZero uses flow matching for action prediction, the teacher and student outputs are velocity fields over continuous action space. Matching velocity fields via MSE:
\[\left\| \mathbf{v}^S_t - \mathbf{v}^T_t \right\|^2\]
is equivalent to minimizing the KL divergence between the implied flow distributions, and integrates natively with DreamZero’s existing training infrastructure.
Stop-gradient on teacher (\(\text{sg}\)). We apply stop-gradient to the teacher’s velocity field to stabilize training, following OPSD. The teacher still improves through \(\mathcal{L}_{\text{video}}\) on cross-embodiment data and \(\mathcal{L}_{\text{action}}\) on target data. An ablation without stop-gradient (joint gradient flow through both modes) may benefit early training when the teacher’s rationalizations are poor.
On-policy rollouts. The student generates its own trajectories, and the teacher provides corrections at the states the student actually visits. This avoids the distribution mismatch problem of off-policy distillation: if the student drifts into an unusual state, the teacher corrects it there, not at states from an expert trajectory the student may never reach.
Single model, gated privileged information. Using a single model with \(\alpha\)-gating rather than separate teacher/student networks means: (a) no extra memory or parameters, (b) improvements to the backbone benefit both modes simultaneously, and (c) the framework is compatible with \(\pi\)-Distill-style joint training schedules that anneal \(\alpha\) over time.

title: On-Policy Self-Distillation for Cross-Embodiment Transfer in World Action Models
text:
4. Experimental Plan

4.1 Setup

Target embodiment: AgiBot (7-DoF manipulator)
Cross-embodiment sources: Human demonstrations (video only), YAM robot data (video only), other robot platforms
\(\mathcal{D}_{\text{tgt}}\): AgiBot teleoperation data (scarce: 10–60 min per task)
\(\mathcal{D}_{\text{cross}}\): Human/YAM video demonstrations (abundant: hours of video)



4.2 Baselines

DreamZero (video-only): Standard co-training with \(\mathcal{L}_{\text{video}}\) on cross-embodiment data. No action supervision from \(\mathcal{D}_{\text{cross}}\).
Off-policy distillation: Teacher generates target-embodiment actions from demos; student imitates via SFT on teacher-generated data (not on-policy).
Target-only: Train only on \(\mathcal{D}_{\text{tgt}}\), no cross-embodiment data. Lower bound.
Action co-training: Train action loss on all embodiments with a learned action-space mapping. Upper bound (requires action labels for all embodiments).



4.3 Key Experiments
Experiment 1: Cross-embodiment transfer efficacy. Compare OPSD-CE vs. baselines on held-out tasks that only appear in cross-embodiment data (never demonstrated on AgiBot). This is the cleanest test: can the model do on AgiBot what it only saw a human do?
Experiment 2: Data efficiency scaling. Vary the ratio \(|\mathcal{D}_{\text{tgt}}| / |\mathcal{D}_{\text{cross}}|\). Hypothesis: OPSD-CE shows the largest gains when target-embodiment data is most scarce, precisely the regime that matters in practice.
Experiment 3: Embodiment gap. Test across embodiment pairs with varying morphological similarity: AgiBot ↔︎ YAM (similar grippers), AgiBot ↔︎ Human (different end-effectors), AgiBot ↔︎ bimanual robot (different kinematics). Hypothesis: performance degrades gracefully as the embodiment gap widens.
Experiment 4: Ablations.

On-policy vs. off-policy distillation
Stop-gradient vs. joint gradient flow
Full demo video vs. keyframes vs. trajectory sketch as privileged information
Sensitivity to \(\beta\) (distillation coefficient)



4.4 Evaluation Metrics

Task success rate on held-out tasks (primary)
Success rate on cross-embodiment-only tasks (zero-shot transfer)
Sample efficiency: success rate as a function of target-embodiment data quantity
Video-action alignment: how well predicted actions match generated video plans

title: On-Policy Self-Distillation for Cross-Embodiment Transfer in World Action Models
text:
5. Expected Contributions

A principled framework for using cross-embodiment demonstrations as privileged information in WAMs, moving beyond video-only training objectives.
Dense action supervision from unlabeled video: cross-embodiment demonstrations that lack target-embodiment action labels are transformed into per-timestep action training signals via self-distillation.
On-policy training for robustness: corrections occur at states the student actually visits, avoiding the compounding errors of off-policy approaches.
Zero additional parameters: the method uses a single model with gated privileged information, requiring no architectural changes to the WAM.

The broader significance is that OPSD-CE turns any cross-embodiment video demonstration into a source of dense action supervision for a target robot—a capability that scales naturally with the growing availability of internet-scale manipulation video.

title: On-Policy Self-Distillation for Cross-Embodiment Transfer in World Action Models
text:
6. Connections to Prior Work
Privileged information distillation. Learning Using Privileged Information (LUPI) trains a teacher with access to information unavailable at test time, then distills to a student. In robotics, this has been applied with access to simulator state, but not with cross-embodiment demonstrations as the privileged modality.
Cross-embodiment transfer. Existing approaches include domain randomization, embodiment-invariant representations, and video-only co-training (as in DreamZero). OPSD-CE is distinct in that it generates action-level supervision from cross-embodiment video, rather than relying solely on shared visual representations.
Self-distillation for reasoning. OPSD and \(\pi\)-Distill show that dense self-distillation from privileged information dramatically improves sample efficiency in language model reasoning. We adapt this insight to the visuomotor domain, where the “reasoning” is motor planning and the “privileged information” is a demonstration from another embodiment.

title: On-Policy Self-Distillation for Cross-Embodiment Transfer in World Action Models
text:
7. Timeline







Phase
Duration

2 weeks

2 weeks

3 weeks

The parallel methods (population, MoD) never forget because specialists never train on each other’s tasks. The simultaneous method (multi-objective) never forgets because all tasks are optimized together. The sequential methods (rehearsal, teacher reg) accept sequential training but add a maintenance signal to slow forgetting. GLM-5 accepts forgetting and repairs it after the fact.
Code

Abstract
We present VLAF (Video-Language-Action Fusion), a unified framework for language-conditioned robot manipulation that bridges the gap between Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models and Video-Action Models (VAMs). Our framework introduces three architectural variants of increasing sophistication:

VLAF-Gate: Gated fusion combining frozen video and language backbones via learned static weights
VLAF-AR: Autoregressive interleaving that processes video-language token sequences through causal attention for dynamic cross-modal reasoning
VLAF-AR+: Full pipeline with VLA-CLIP trimodal contrastive pretraining followed by autoregressive interleaving

Our key insight is that effective robot learning requires both representational alignment (understanding that “pick up red cup”, the video of picking up a red cup, and the corresponding action trajectory are semantically equivalent) and sequential reasoning (understanding how specific language tokens relate to specific video frames and action phases). VLAF-Gate provides efficient fusion for simple tasks; VLAF-AR adds dynamic reasoning for complex tasks; VLAF-AR+ adds explicit trimodal alignment for maximum performance and zero-shot capabilities.
We hypothesize that VLAF-AR+ will achieve state-of-the-art performance on complex manipulation benchmarks, VLAF-AR will excel on tasks requiring fine-grained cross-modal reasoning, and VLAF-Gate will remain optimal for real-time control scenarios. Together, these variants provide a complete toolkit for language-conditioned robot learning across the full spectrum of task complexity and computational constraints.

1. Introduction

1.1 Motivation
Language-conditioned robot manipulation requires bridging three modalities:

Language (\(\ell\)): Semantic understanding of goals (“pick up the cup behind the red block”)
Video (\(\mathbf{v}\)): Visual perception of scenes, objects, spatial relations, and dynamics
Action (\(a\)): Motor commands that achieve the specified goal

Current approaches handle at most two modalities well:



Approach
Semantics
Dynamics
Cross-Modal Reasoning




VLA (RT-2, OpenVLA)
✓✓
✗
Implicit


VAM (Cosmos, UniVLA)
✗
✓✓
Temporal only


CLIP-based (R3M, VIP)
✓
✓
Static



None provide the full combination of semantic understanding, physical dynamics, and dynamic cross-modal reasoning.


1.2 Key Insights
Insight 1: Complementary Priors
Video models pretrained on internet-scale data capture physics, motion, and dynamics. Language models capture semantics, reasoning, and world knowledge. Neither alone is sufficient; fusion is necessary.
Insight 2: Static vs. Dynamic Fusion
Simple tasks (“pick up the red cup”) may only need static feature combination. Complex tasks (“pick up the cup behind the red block, then place it next to the blue bowl”) require dynamic, sequential reasoning where each word attends to relevant visual regions.
Insight 3: Alignment Before Reasoning
Before learning to reason across modalities, the model should understand that video, language, and action representations of the same concept are equivalent. Contrastive learning provides this foundation.
Insight 4: LLM Training Pipeline for Robotics
Modern LLMs succeed via staged training: pretraining → SFT → RLHF. We adapt this paradigm: - Contrastive pretraining (optional) → Behavioral cloning → RL fine-tuning


1.3 Contributions

Unified VLAF Framework: Three architectural variants spanning the complexity-performance spectrum
VLA-CLIP: First trimodal contrastive objective aligning video, language, and action
Autoregressive Interleaving: Dynamic cross-modal reasoning via causal attention
Comprehensive Analysis: Theoretical and empirical comparison of fusion strategies

2. Problem Formulation

2.1 Notation







Symbol
Definition




\(s_t = (o_t, p_t)\)
State: visual observation \(o_t \in \mathbb{R}^{H \times W \times 3}\) + proprioception \(p_t \in \mathbb{R}^{d_p}\)


\(\mathbf{v}_{1:T}\)
Video sequence \((v_1, \ldots, v_T)\)


\(\ell \in \mathcal{V}^*\)
Language instruction (token sequence)


\(a \in \mathcal{A} \subseteq \mathbb{R}^{d_a}\)
Action (typically 7-DoF: 6-DoF pose + gripper)


\(\pi_\theta\)
Policy parameterized by \(\theta\)





2.2 Objective
Learn a policy maximizing expected return under sparse rewards:
\[\pi^* = \arg\max_\theta \mathbb{E}_{\tau \sim \pi_\theta} \left[ \sum_{t=0}^{H} \gamma^t r(s_t, a_t, \ell) \right]\]
where \(r \in \{0, 1\}\) indicates task success and \(\gamma \in [0, 1)\) is the discount factor.


2.3 Challenges

Semantic Grounding: Mapping language concepts to visual entities
Spatial Reasoning: Understanding relations (“behind”, “next to”, “between”)
Temporal Alignment: Coordinating language with action phases
Physical Dynamics: Predicting motion and contact outcomes
Sample Efficiency: Learning from limited robot demonstrations

4. VLAF-Gate: Gated Fusion

4.1 Architecture
VLAF-Gate combines frozen video and language backbones via a learned gating mechanism.
        Video v                    Language ℓ
           │                            │
           ▼                            ▼
    ┌─────────────┐              ┌─────────────┐
    │   Video     │              │  Language   │
    │  Encoder    │              │  Encoder    │
    │  (Frozen)   │              │  (Frozen)   │
    └──────┬──────┘              └──────┬──────┘
           │                            │
           │ h_v ∈ ℝ^d_v                │ h_ℓ ∈ ℝ^d_ℓ
           │                            │
           └──────────┬─────────────────┘
                      │
                      ▼
              ┌──────────────┐
              │    Gated     │
              │   Fusion     │
              │  (Learned)   │
              └──────┬───────┘
                     │
                     │ h ∈ ℝ^d
                     ▼
              ┌──────────────┐
              │   Action     │
              │   Decoder    │
              └──────┬───────┘
                     │
                     ▼
                  Action a


4.2 Components
Video Encoder \(f_v\): Cosmos-Predict2 DiT or VideoMAE
\[h_v = \text{Pool}(f_v(\mathbf{v}_{1:T})) \in \mathbb{R}^{d_v}\]
Pretrained with video diffusion:
\[\mathcal{L}_{\text{video}}^{\text{pretrain}} = \mathbb{E}_{t, \epsilon, \mathbf{v}} \left[ \| \epsilon - \epsilon_\phi(\sqrt{\bar{\alpha}_t} \mathbf{v} + \sqrt{1-\bar{\alpha}_t} \epsilon, t) \|^2 \right]\]
Language Encoder \(f_\ell\): Llama-3 or CLIP text encoder
\[h_\ell = \text{Pool}(f_\ell(\ell)) \in \mathbb{R}^{d_\ell}\]
Pretrained with autoregressive language modeling:
\[\mathcal{L}_{\text{LM}}^{\text{pretrain}} = -\mathbb{E}_{\ell} \left[ \sum_{i=1}^{L} \log p_\phi(w_i | w_{<i}) \right]\]
Gated Fusion Module \(g_\phi\):
\[h = g_\phi(h_v, h_\ell) = \alpha \odot \tilde{h}_v + (1 - \alpha) \odot \tilde{h}_\ell\]
where:
\[\tilde{h}_v = W_v h_v + b_v \in \mathbb{R}^d\] \[\tilde{h}_\ell = W_\ell h_\ell + b_\ell \in \mathbb{R}^d\] \[\alpha = \sigma(W_g [h_v; h_\ell] + b_g) \in (0, 1)^d\]
The gating weights \(\alpha\) are learned and determine how much to rely on video vs. language features.
Action Decoder \(\pi_\theta\):
\[a = \pi_\theta(h, p) \in \mathcal{A}\]
Options: MLP, Flow Matching, or Diffusion Policy.


4.3 Training
Stage 1: Backbone Initialization - Load pretrained Cosmos and Llama weights - Freeze both encoders
Stage 2: Behavioral Cloning
\[\mathcal{L}_{\text{Gate-BC}} = \mathbb{E}_{(\mathbf{v}, \ell, p, a^*) \sim \mathcal{D}} \left[ \| \pi_\theta(g_\phi(f_v(\mathbf{v}), f_\ell(\ell)), p) - a^* \|^2 \right]\]
Trainable: Fusion module \(\phi\), action decoder \(\theta\)
Stage 3: RL Fine-tuning
\[J(\theta, \phi) = \mathbb{E}_{\tau \sim \pi} \left[ \sum_{t=0}^{H} \gamma^t r(s_t, a_t) \right]\]

5. VLAF-AR: Autoregressive Interleaving

5.1 Motivation
VLAF-Gate uses static fusion—the gating weights don’t depend on the specific content of video or language. For complex instructions like “pick up the cup behind the red block”, we need dynamic attention where:

“cup” attends to cup-like regions in video
“behind” triggers spatial reasoning
“red block” filters by spatial constraint

VLAF-AR achieves this via autoregressive modeling over interleaved sequences.

5.3 Components
Token Extraction (from frozen encoders):
\[z_v^{\text{seq}} = f_v(\mathbf{v}_{1:T}) \in \mathbb{R}^{M \times d}\] \[z_\ell^{\text{seq}} = f_\ell(\ell) \in \mathbb{R}^{N \times d}\]
Interleaved Sequence Construction:
\[x = \text{Interleave}(z_v^{\text{seq}}, z_\ell^{\text{seq}}) \oplus [\text{ACT}] \oplus \text{Embed}(p)\]
Explicitly:
\[x = [v_1, \ell_1, v_2, \ell_2, \ldots, v_{\min(M,N)}, \ell_{\min(M,N)}, \ldots, [\text{ACT}], p]\]
Positional and Modality Embeddings:
\[\tilde{x}_i = x_i + e_{\text{pos}}(i) + e_{\text{mod}}(m_i)\]
where \(m_i \in \{\texttt{video}, \texttt{language}, \texttt{action}, \texttt{proprio}\}\).
Autoregressive Transformer:
\[h_t = \text{Transformer}(x_{\leq t})\]
With causal self-attention:
\[\text{Attn}(Q, K, V) = \text{softmax}\left( \frac{QK^\top}{\sqrt{d_k}} + M_{\text{causal}} \right) V\]
where \(M_{\text{causal}}\) masks future positions.
Action Decoder:
\[a_{t:t+k} = \pi_\theta(h_{[\text{ACT}]})\]


5.4 Training Objectives
Action Loss (primary):
\[\mathcal{L}_{\text{action}} = \mathbb{E}_{(\mathbf{v}, \ell, p, a^*) \sim \mathcal{D}} \left[ \| \pi_\theta(h_{[\text{ACT}]}) - a^* \|^2 \right]\]
Autoregressive Loss (auxiliary):
\[\mathcal{L}_{\text{AR}} = -\sum_{t=1}^{|x|-1} \log p_\theta(x_{t+1} | h_t)\]
Since video and language tokens may be in different embedding spaces, we use modality-specific prediction heads:
\[\mathcal{L}_{\text{AR}} = \underbrace{\sum_{t: x_{t+1} \in \mathcal{V}} \| g_\theta^{(v)}(h_t) - x_{t+1} \|^2}_{\text{video token prediction}} + \underbrace{\left( -\sum_{t: x_{t+1} \in \mathcal{L}} \log p_\theta^{(\ell)}(x_{t+1} | h_t) \right)}_{\text{language token prediction}}\]
Auxiliary Video Prediction (optional):
\[\mathcal{L}_{\text{aux}} = \| \text{Decode}(h_{v_t}) - v_{t+1} \|^2\]
Total Loss:
\[\boxed{\mathcal{L}_{\text{VLAF-AR}} = \mathcal{L}_{\text{action}} + \lambda_1 \mathcal{L}_{\text{AR}} + \lambda_2 \mathcal{L}_{\text{aux}}}\]


5.5 Why AR Loss Helps
The autoregressive objective forces cross-modal grounding:

To predict the next language token, attend to relevant video context
To predict the next video token, understand the linguistic context
This creates self-supervised signal for alignment

6. VLAF-AR+: Contrastive Pretraining + Autoregressive Interleaving

6.1 Motivation
VLAF-AR must learn cross-modal alignment and sequential reasoning simultaneously. This is challenging because video and language tokens start in different embedding spaces.
VLAF-AR+ adds a contrastive pretraining stage (VLA-CLIP) that aligns video, language, and action in a shared space before the AR transformer sees them. This provides:

Better initialization: Tokens are pre-aligned
Simpler AR objective: Unified prediction (no separate heads)
Zero-shot capabilities: Action retrieval via embedding similarity

6.3 VLA-CLIP: Trimodal Contrastive Pretraining
Encoders:
\[h_v = \text{Pool}(f_v(\mathbf{v})) \in \mathbb{R}^{d_v}\] \[h_\ell = \text{Pool}(f_\ell(\ell)) \in \mathbb{R}^{d_\ell}\] \[h_a = f_a(a_{1:k}) \in \mathbb{R}^{d_a}\]
Projection Heads (to shared space):
\[z_v = \frac{W_v h_v + b_v}{\|W_v h_v + b_v\|_2} \in \mathbb{R}^d\] \[z_\ell = \frac{W_\ell h_\ell + b_\ell}{\|W_\ell h_\ell + b_\ell\|_2} \in \mathbb{R}^d\] \[z_a = \frac{W_a h_a + b_a}{\|W_a h_a + b_a\|_2} \in \mathbb{R}^d\]
Contrastive Losses (symmetric InfoNCE):
\[\mathcal{L}_{\text{V-L}} = -\frac{1}{2B} \sum_{i=1}^{B} \left[ \log \frac{\exp(z_{v,i}^\top z_{\ell,i} / \tau)}{\sum_{j=1}^{B} \exp(z_{v,i}^\top z_{\ell,j} / \tau)} + \log \frac{\exp(z_{\ell,i}^\top z_{v,i} / \tau)}{\sum_{j=1}^{B} \exp(z_{\ell,i}^\top z_{v,j} / \tau)} \right]\]
\[\mathcal{L}_{\text{V-A}} = -\frac{1}{2B} \sum_{i=1}^{B} \left[ \log \frac{\exp(z_{v,i}^\top z_{a,i} / \tau)}{\sum_{j=1}^{B} \exp(z_{v,i}^\top z_{a,j} / \tau)} + \log \frac{\exp(z_{a,i}^\top z_{v,i} / \tau)}{\sum_{j=1}^{B} \exp(z_{a,i}^\top z_{v,j} / \tau)} \right]\]
\[\mathcal{L}_{\text{L-A}} = -\frac{1}{2B} \sum_{i=1}^{B} \left[ \log \frac{\exp(z_{\ell,i}^\top z_{a,i} / \tau)}{\sum_{j=1}^{B} \exp(z_{\ell,i}^\top z_{a,j} / \tau)} + \log \frac{\exp(z_{a,i}^\top z_{\ell,i} / \tau)}{\sum_{j=1}^{B} \exp(z_{a,i}^\top z_{\ell,j} / \tau)} \right]\]
Total VLA-CLIP Loss:
\[\boxed{\mathcal{L}_{\text{VLA-CLIP}} = \mathcal{L}_{\text{V-L}} + \mathcal{L}_{\text{V-A}} + \mathcal{L}_{\text{L-A}}}\]


6.4 Simplified AR Training
Because tokens are now in the same VLA-CLIP space, the AR objective simplifies:
\[\mathcal{L}_{\text{AR}}^{\text{unified}} = -\sum_{t=1}^{|x|-1} \log \frac{\exp(h_t^\top x_{t+1} / \tau)}{\sum_{j} \exp(h_t^\top x_j / \tau)}\]
This is contrastive next-token prediction: pull \(h_t\) toward the true next token, push away from other tokens in the batch. No need for separate video/language heads!
Total VLAF-AR+ Loss (Phase 2):
\[\boxed{\mathcal{L}_{\text{VLAF-AR+}} = \mathcal{L}_{\text{action}} + \lambda_1 \mathcal{L}_{\text{AR}}^{\text{unified}} + \lambda_2 \mathcal{L}_{\text{aux}}}\]


6.5 Zero-Shot Capabilities
VLA-CLIP enables zero-shot action retrieval:
Given instruction, retrieve action: \[a^* = \arg\max_{a \in \mathcal{A}_{\text{database}}} z_\ell^\top z_a\]
Given video, retrieve action: \[a^* = \arg\max_{a \in \mathcal{A}_{\text{database}}} z_v^\top z_a\]
Cross-task transfer: Tasks with similar language descriptions have similar action embeddings.

8.2 Information-Theoretic View
VLA-CLIP maximizes mutual information between modalities:
\[\mathcal{L}_{\text{VLA-CLIP}} \propto -I(Z_v; Z_\ell) - I(Z_v; Z_a) - I(Z_\ell; Z_a)\]
AR objective captures conditional dependencies:
\[\mathcal{L}_{\text{AR}} \propto -I(X_{t+1}; X_{\leq t})\]
Together, they capture global alignment (VLA-CLIP) and local sequential dependencies (AR).


8.3 Sample Complexity
Since backbones are frozen, trainable parameters are reduced:
\[d_{\text{trainable}} = d_{\text{fusion/AR}} + d_{\text{decoder}} \ll d_{\text{full}}\]
This yields improved sample complexity:
\[\mathbb{E}[\mathcal{L}(\hat{\theta})] - \mathcal{L}(\theta^*) \leq O\left( \frac{d_{\text{trainable}}}{N} \right)\]
VLA-CLIP further improves sample efficiency by leveraging unpaired data for alignment.


8.4 Emergent Reasoning in VLAF-AR
The autoregressive structure enables chain-of-thought-like reasoning. For “pick up the cup behind the red block”:

Process “pick up” → attend to grasping-relevant video features
Process “cup” → identify cup candidates
Process “behind” → compute spatial relations
Process “red block” → filter by spatial constraint
\([\text{ACT}]\) → aggregate into action

This sequential reasoning is implicit in gated fusion but explicit in AR variants.

9.3 Pseudocode
VLA-CLIP Forward Pass:
def vla_clip_forward(video, language, action):
    # Encode
    h_v = video_encoder(video)       # [B, d_v]
    h_l = language_encoder(language) # [B, d_l]
    h_a = action_encoder(action)     # [B, d_a]
    
    # Project and normalize
    z_v = F.normalize(proj_v(h_v), dim=-1)  # [B, d]
    z_l = F.normalize(proj_l(h_l), dim=-1)  # [B, d]
    z_a = F.normalize(proj_a(h_a), dim=-1)  # [B, d]
    
    # InfoNCE losses
    loss_vl = info_nce(z_v, z_l, tau)
    loss_va = info_nce(z_v, z_a, tau)
    loss_la = info_nce(z_l, z_a, tau)
    
    return loss_vl + loss_va + loss_la
VLAF-AR Forward Pass:
def vlaf_ar_forward(video, language, proprio, action_gt):
    # Get token sequences from frozen encoders
    z_v = frozen_video_encoder(video)      # [B, M, d]
    z_l = frozen_language_encoder(language) # [B, N, d]
    
    # Interleave and add special tokens
    x = interleave(z_v, z_l)               # [B, M+N, d]
    x = concat([x, act_token, embed(proprio)], dim=1)
    
    # AR transformer with causal mask
    h = ar_transformer(x)                   # [B, M+N+2, d]
    
    # Action prediction
    h_act = h[:, -2, :]                     # [B, d]
    a_pred = action_decoder(h_act)          # [B, k, d_a]
    
    # Losses
    loss_action = mse_loss(a_pred, action_gt)
    loss_ar = ar_loss(h[:, :-1], x[:, 1:])
    
    return loss_action + lambda1 * loss_ar

Use VLAF-AR+ when: - Maximum performance is the goal - Zero-shot transfer or retrieval is needed - You have resources for contrastive pretraining - Cross-task generalization matters

13. Hypotheses
H1 (Fusion Strategy): VLAF-AR+ > VLAF-AR > VLAF-Gate on tasks requiring complex spatial reasoning.
H2 (Contrastive Pretraining): VLA-CLIP pretraining improves downstream performance by ≥10% and enables zero-shot action retrieval with ≥60% top-1 accuracy.
H3 (AR Objective): The autoregressive loss improves cross-modal grounding, measurable via attention pattern analysis.
H4 (Sample Efficiency): VLAF-AR+ achieves equivalent performance to VLAF-AR with 50% fewer demonstrations due to better initialization.
H5 (Latency-Accuracy Tradeoff): VLAF-Gate maintains ≤50ms latency while VLAF-AR/AR+ achieve higher accuracy at ~150ms.
H6 (Interleaving): Interleaving outperforms concatenation by ≥15% on complex tasks due to fine-grained cross-modal attention.

14. Limitations and Future Work

14.1 Limitations

Compute Cost: \(O((M+N)^2)\) attention limits real-time deployment for AR variants
Data Requirements: VLA-CLIP benefits from large-scale paired and unpaired data
Modality Gap: Some gap may remain despite contrastive learning
Latency: AR variants are ~3x slower than gated fusion



14.2 Future Directions

Sparse Attention: Reduce complexity to \(O((M+N) \log(M+N))\)
Distillation: Compress VLAF-AR+ to VLAF-Gate-sized model
Additional Modalities: Add tactile, audio, depth to VLA-CLIP
Hierarchical Contrastive: Frame-level + trajectory-level alignment
Self-Supervised Actions: Learn action encoder without labeled actions
Adaptive Routing: Dynamically choose Gate vs. AR based on task complexity

15. Conclusion
We presented VLAF, a unified framework for language-conditioned robot manipulation with three architectural variants:

VLAF-Gate: Efficient gated fusion for real-time control
VLAF-AR: Autoregressive interleaving for dynamic cross-modal reasoning
VLAF-AR+: Trimodal contrastive pretraining + AR for maximum performance

Our key contributions are:

VLA-CLIP: First trimodal contrastive objective for robotics (V ↔︎ L ↔︎ A)
Autoregressive Interleaving: Dynamic attention over interleaved video-language tokens
Unified Pipeline: Staged training from pretraining to RL fine-tuning
Comprehensive Analysis: Theoretical and empirical comparison of fusion strategies

The VLAF framework provides a complete toolkit spanning the complexity-performance spectrum. VLAF-Gate serves real-time applications; VLAF-AR handles complex reasoning; VLAF-AR+ achieves maximum performance with zero-shot capabilities. Together, they advance the state-of-the-art in language-conditioned robot learning.

\[\mathcal{L}_{\text{AR}} = \underbrace{\| \pi_\theta(h_{[\text{ACT}]}) - a^* \|^2}_{\text{action}} + \lambda_1 \underbrace{\left( \sum_{t \in \mathcal{V}} \|g^{(v)}(h_t) - x_{t+1}\|^2 - \sum_{t \in \mathcal{L}} \log p^{(\ell)}(x_{t+1}|h_t) \right)}_{\text{AR (separate heads)}} + \lambda_2 \underbrace{\|\hat{v}_{t+1} - v_{t+1}\|^2}_{\text{aux}}\]

\[J(\theta) = \mathbb{E}_{\tau \sim \pi_\theta} \left[ \sum_{t=0}^{H} \gamma^t r(s_t, a_t) \right], \quad r \in \{0, 1\}\]

title: VLAF: Video-Language-Action Fusion for Robot Learning Backbone
text:
Main idea is to combine VLM backbone for semantics, with video model backbone for physics/dynamics as a unified prior for downstream tasks on robot learning.
Combining VLA semantic understanding with video model dynamics priors for sample-efficient, language-conditioned robot manipulation.

title: VLAF: Video-Language-Action Fusion for Robot Learning Backbone
text:
Motivation
Recent advances in robot learning have produced two powerful but complementary paradigms:









VLAs (e.g., OpenVLA, π₀)
Video-Action Models (e.g., Cosmos Policy, UVA)




Strength
Rich semantic grounding from VLMs — understands which object, spatial relations, task goals
Temporal dynamics from video pretraining — understands how things move, physics, motion patterns


Weakness
No dynamics prior; treats each frame independently
Weak semantic grounding; typically uses lightweight text encoders (T5), not full VLMs



VLAF bridges this gap. Rather than training a single monolithic model from scratch, VLAF fuses two frozen pretrained backbones — a vision-language model and a video diffusion model — through a lightweight, trainable gated cross-attention module.

title: VLAF: Video-Language-Action Fusion for Robot Learning Backbone
text:
Key Idea
VLAs have semantics but lack dynamics.
Video-Action Models have dynamics but lack semantics.
VLAF fuses them.
VLAF keeps both backbones frozen and trains only a small fusion module (~60M params) and action decoder, preserving the pretrained capabilities of each while learning to combine them. A learned gate adaptively weights semantic vs. dynamics features per-task: pure semantic attention for object selection, pure dynamics for motion execution, balanced for complex manipulation.

title: VLAF: Video-Language-Action Fusion for Robot Learning Backbone
text:
Architecture
VLAF processes four input streams — video frames, language instructions, robot state, and proprioception — through two parallel frozen branches:
Language Branch (Llama-3 / VLM): Encodes the current image and language goal into semantic features capturing object identities, spatial relations, and task understanding.
Video Branch (Cosmos-Predict2 DiT): Encodes video history into dynamics features capturing motion patterns, physics priors, and temporal predictions.
A Gated Cross-Attention Fusion Module combines these representations:
\[z_{\text{fused}} = \sigma(W) \cdot h_{\text{semantic}} + (1 - \sigma(W)) \cdot h_{\text{dynamics}}\]
The fused representation feeds into a Flow Matching Action Decoder that outputs action chunks (end-effector trajectories).

title: VLAF: Video-Language-Action Fusion for Robot Learning Backbone
text:
Training Pipeline
VLAF follows a three-stage pipeline analogous to the modern LLM training recipe:

Stage 1: Pre-training (Frozen Backbones)
Load pretrained video encoder (Cosmos-Predict2) and language model (Llama-3). Both remain frozen throughout training, preserving their respective capabilities.
Analogy: LLM pre-training — large-scale representation learning.


Stage 2: Behavioral Cloning (Mid-training)
Attach a trainable action decoder and fusion module. Train on large-scale robot demonstration datasets (Open X-Embodiment, DROID) with MSE loss to bake in cross-task and cross-embodiment generalization.
Analogy: LLM supervised fine-tuning (SFT) — adapting to the target domain.


Stage 3: RL Fine-tuning (Post-training)
Fine-tune on a specific target task using on-policy or off-policy RL (TD3/SAC) with sparse rewards (success/failure). Only the action head and optional LoRA adapters are updated. This stage is designed to be sample-efficient (few-shot) because the video backbone provides dynamics priors, the LLM provides semantic priors, and the BC phase provides manipulation priors.
Analogy: RLHF/RLVR — task-specific alignment.
┌─────────────────┐     ┌─────────────────┐     ┌─────────────────┐
│  Stage 1:       │     │  Stage 2:       │     │  Stage 3:       │
│  Pre-training   │────▶│  Behavioral     │────▶│  RL Fine-tuning │
│                 │     │  Cloning        │     │                 │
│  Frozen video + │     │  Action decoder │     │  On-policy RL   │
│  language       │     │  + fusion on    │     │  on target task │
│  backbones      │     │  robot demos    │     │  (few-shot)     │
└─────────────────┘     └─────────────────┘     └─────────────────┘
  ≈ LLM Pre-train        ≈ LLM SFT              ≈ RLHF / RLVR

title: VLAF: Video-Language-Action Fusion for Robot Learning Backbone
text:
Algorithm
Algorithm: VLAF Training Pipeline
─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
Require: Pretrained f_v (video encoder), f_ℓ (language model)
         BC dataset D_BC; Target environment E

Stage 1: Load frozen backbones
  f_v ← Cosmos-Predict2,  f_ℓ ← Llama-3

Stage 2: Behavioral Cloning
  Initialize fusion module g_φ and action decoder π_θ
  for each batch (v, ℓ, s, a*) ~ D_BC:
      h_v ← f_v(v)                    // frozen forward pass
      h_ℓ ← f_ℓ(ℓ)                    // frozen forward pass
      h   ← g_φ(h_v, h_ℓ; φ)         // trainable fusion
      â   ← π_θ(h)                    // trainable action decoder
      L   ← ‖â - a*‖²
      Update φ, θ via gradient descent on L

Stage 3: RL Fine-tuning
  for each episode:
      Collect rollouts with π_θ in E
      Store (s, a, r, s') in replay buffer B
      Update θ (and optionally φ) via SAC/TD3 on B

Return: π_θ

title: VLAF: Video-Language-Action Fusion for Robot Learning Backbone
text:
Design Decisions & Open Questions
Why explicit fusion over unified autoregressive? Concurrent work like UniVLA uses a single autoregressive transformer that interleaves video prediction and action prediction. VLAF instead keeps branches separate and fuses post-hoc. The bet is that two pretrained specialists outperform one jointly-trained generalist when robot data is limited (1K–10K demos). Whether this holds is an open empirical question.
Why frozen backbones? Freezing preserves the full pretrained capability of each model and reduces trainable parameters to ~60M (fusion + decoder only). This enables training on a single GPU and avoids catastrophic forgetting.
Video prediction as auxiliary loss. UniVLA shows that world model post-training (training on video prediction without actions) dramatically improves downstream policy learning. VLAF could incorporate this by adding an auxiliary video prediction loss during BC.
Deployment considerations. At inference, the video backbone can potentially be distilled away — the action decoder may have the dynamics representation “baked in” from training. On-policy distillation could prune the system to meet real-time control requirements (~20 Hz).

title: VLAF: Video-Language-Action Fusion for Robot Learning Backbone
text:
Positioning in the Landscape
The current video-action model space has two main camps:
Camp 1 — Autoregressive Interleaving: Predict video → predict action → repeat. (UniVLA [1], UVA [4])
Camp 2 — Explicit Branch Fusion: Maintain separate modality encoders, fuse via cross-attention or gating. (VLAF, this work)
A key distinction from prior work: many papers claiming “video + language” fusion simply use a T5 text encoder projected into the video model’s feature space [5]. VLAF uses a full VLM backbone (Llama-3), preserving the rich semantic and reasoning capabilities that make VLAs powerful.

Key hypotheses to test: 1. VLAF (fusion) > VLA alone and > VAM alone on language-conditioned tasks 2. VLAF is competitive with unified autoregressive approaches (UniVLA) 3. The gating mechanism learns interpretable task-dependent weighting 4. RL fine-tuning is sample-efficient (<100 episodes) due to strong priors

title: VLAF: Video-Language-Action Fusion for Robot Learning Backbone
text:
Minimum Viable Experiment
The smallest experiment that validates whether VLAF has legs:

Take OpenVLA features (VLA baseline)
Take mimic-video features (video baseline)
Concatenate naively and train an action decoder
Compare to each alone on LIBERO

If naive concatenation improves over either branch alone, the fusion hypothesis is validated and the full gated cross-attention architecture is worth pursuing.

title: VLAF: Video-Language-Action Fusion for Robot Learning Backbone
text:
Pseudocode
"""
VLAF: Video-Language-Action Fusion

Core architecture: frozen VLM + frozen video model → gated cross-attention fusion → action decoder.
"""

import torch
import torch.nn as nn


class GatedCrossAttentionFusion(nn.Module):
    """Learnable gated cross-attention module that fuses semantic and dynamics features.
    
    The gate σ(W) ∈ [0, 1] controls the relative influence of dynamics vs. semantic features:
        z_fused = σ(W) · CrossAttn(h_sem, h_dyn) + (1 - σ(W)) · h_sem
    
    When g → 0: pure semantic (object selection tasks)
    When g → 1: pure dynamics (motion execution tasks)  
    When g ≈ 0.5: balanced (complex manipulation)
    """

    def __init__(self, d_model: int = 512, n_heads: int = 8, dropout: float = 0.1):
        super().__init__()
        self.cross_attn = nn.MultiheadAttention(d_model, n_heads, dropout=dropout, batch_first=True)
        self.gate = nn.Parameter(torch.zeros(1))  # initialized to σ(0) = 0.5
        self.proj_semantic = nn.Linear(d_model, d_model)
        self.proj_dynamics = nn.Linear(d_model, d_model)
        self.layer_norm = nn.LayerNorm(d_model)

    def forward(self, h_semantic: torch.Tensor, h_dynamics: torch.Tensor) -> torch.Tensor:
        """
        Args:
            h_semantic: (B, N, d) — VLM features (object identities, spatial relations, goals)
            h_dynamics: (B, M, d) — Video model features (motion patterns, physics priors)
        
        Returns:
            h_fused: (B, N, d) — fused representation
        """
        h_sem = self.proj_semantic(h_semantic)
        h_dyn = self.proj_dynamics(h_dynamics)

        # Cross-attention: semantic queries attend to dynamics keys/values
        cross_out, _ = self.cross_attn(query=h_sem, key=h_dyn, value=h_dyn)

        # Gated fusion
        g = torch.sigmoid(self.gate)
        h_fused = g * cross_out + (1 - g) * h_sem

        return self.layer_norm(h_fused)


class ActionDecoder(nn.Module):
    """MLP action decoder that maps fused features + proprioception to action chunks.
    
    For the full system, this would be replaced with a Flow Matching network.
    """

    def __init__(
        self,
        d_fused: int = 512,
        d_proprio: int = 14,
        d_action: int = 7,
        chunk_size: int = 8,
        hidden_dim: int = 256,
    ):
        super().__init__()
        self.chunk_size = chunk_size
        self.net = nn.Sequential(
            nn.Linear(d_fused + d_proprio, hidden_dim),
            nn.ReLU(),
            nn.Linear(hidden_dim, hidden_dim),
            nn.ReLU(),
            nn.Linear(hidden_dim, d_action * chunk_size),
        )

    def forward(self, h_fused: torch.Tensor, q: torch.Tensor) -> torch.Tensor:
        """
        Args:
            h_fused: (B, d) — pooled fused features
            q: (B, d_proprio) — proprioceptive state
        
        Returns:
            actions: (B, chunk_size, d_action) — predicted action chunk
        """
        x = torch.cat([h_fused, q], dim=-1)
        out = self.net(x)
        return out.view(-1, self.chunk_size, out.shape[-1] // self.chunk_size)


class VLAF(nn.Module):
    """Video-Language-Action Fusion model.
    
    Architecture:
        1. Frozen video backbone → h_dynamics
        2. Frozen VLM backbone → h_semantic
        3. Gated cross-attention fusion → h_fused
        4. Action decoder → action chunks
    
    Only the fusion module and action decoder are trained.
    """

    def __init__(
        self,
        video_backbone: nn.Module,
        language_backbone: nn.Module,
        d_model: int = 512,
        d_proprio: int = 14,
        d_action: int = 7,
        chunk_size: int = 8,
    ):
        super().__init__()

        # Frozen backbones (no gradients)
        self.video_backbone = video_backbone
        self.language_backbone = language_backbone
        for param in self.video_backbone.parameters():
            param.requires_grad = False
        for param in self.language_backbone.parameters():
            param.requires_grad = False

        # Trainable components
        self.fusion = GatedCrossAttentionFusion(d_model=d_model)
        self.action_decoder = ActionDecoder(
            d_fused=d_model,
            d_proprio=d_proprio,
            d_action=d_action,
            chunk_size=chunk_size,
        )

    def forward(
        self,
        video: torch.Tensor,
        language: torch.Tensor,
        image: torch.Tensor,
        proprio: torch.Tensor,
    ) -> torch.Tensor:
        """
        Args:
            video: (B, T, C, H, W) — video history
            language: (B, L) — tokenized language instruction
            image: (B, C, H, W) — current frame
            proprio: (B, d_proprio) — proprioceptive state
        
        Returns:
            actions: (B, chunk_size, d_action) — predicted action chunk
        """
        with torch.no_grad():
            h_dynamics = self.video_backbone(video)          # (B, M, d)
            h_semantic = self.language_backbone(image, language)  # (B, N, d)

        h_fused = self.fusion(h_semantic, h_dynamics)        # (B, N, d)
        h_pooled = h_fused.mean(dim=1)                       # (B, d)

        actions = self.action_decoder(h_pooled, proprio)     # (B, k, d_a)
        return actions

    @property
    def trainable_params(self) -> int:
        return sum(p.numel() for p in self.parameters() if p.requires_grad)

    @property
    def total_params(self) -> int:
        return sum(p.numel() for p in self.parameters())

    def get_gate_value(self) -> float:
        """Returns the current gate value σ(W) for interpretability analysis."""
        return torch.sigmoid(self.fusion.gate).item()







Notes/brainstorm




Main design decision here is you do the fusion between the two modalities (video, language). Late fusion post-encoding? pre-encoding fusion? No fusion and do autoregressive interleaving instead (UVA/univla-style)?
DreamZero paper, and some of the cosmos paper, seem to be validating lots of the video model hyphotheses in general.
For the video model, there are two approaches: train it such that the loss is simple next frame prediction (i.e., true self-supervised) or train it such that it predicts the action, given the video frame(s) (i.e., the world modeling approach).
One thought: we can use a clip-style contrastive objective, to pair them together. let’s say you have {video, language, action} or some combination of them thereof, train a CLIP-style contrastive learning objective as your backbone.
3 variants so far:

VLAF-Fusion (standard VLAF fusion)
VLA-AR (interleaving autoregressive)
VLA-Contrastive (like number 2 but use contrastive learning)

title: Search Self-Distillation
text:
“generalized reasoning engine”. By replacing standard gradient steps with search in the inner loop, we avoid the trap of just doing Supervised Fine-Tuning. The teacher acts as the search heuristic, and the student compiles those discoveries back into its base weights, staying strictly on-policy to prevent distribution shift.
This formulation heavily utilizes the latent reasoning tokens to bridge the gap between exploration and distillation, clarifying the model’s intent at each step.

1. The Search-Distillation Objective Function
Let the policy be \(\pi_{\theta}\). For a given prompt \(s\), a complete trajectory \(\tau\) consists of a sequence of intermediate reasoning tokens \(r = (r_1, ..., r_k)\) and a final answer/action \(a\). So, \(\tau = (r, a)\).
The Inner Loop (Teacher’s Search): Instead of Test-Time Training via gradients , the Teacher runs a search algorithm \(\mathcal{S}\) (e.g., MCTS or a STaR-like rejection sampling) over the latent reasoning space. The search is guided by the policy’s own prior probabilities and an environment verifier \(V(s, \tau) \in \{0, 1\}\).
The output of the search is a golden trajectory \(\tau^* = (r^*, a^*)\) that successfully solves the prompt:
\[\tau^* = \text{argmax}_{\tau \in \mathcal{S}(\pi_{\theta}, s)} V(s, \tau)\]
The Outer Loop (Student’s Distillation): The Student (which shares the exact same weights \(\theta\)) is then updated to maximize the likelihood of this verifiable reasoning path. To maintain stability in RLVR, we optimize this using a policy gradient method like GRPO, treating the discovered \(\tau^*\) as the behavioral target.
The distillation objective forces the model’s latent representations to align with the successful reasoning trace:
\[\mathcal{L}_{distill}(\theta) = -\mathbb{E}_{s \sim \mathcal{D}, \tau^* \sim \mathcal{S}} \left[ \sum_{t=1}^{|\tau^*|} \log \pi_{\theta}(\tau^*_t | s, \tau^*_{<t}) \right]\]
If you want to maintain the relative advantage framing (like in GRPO), you can structure it as an advantage-weighted loss where trajectories found via search that yield high verifier scores receive a positive advantage \(+A\), and failed searches receive \(-A\).



2. Algorithm: Generalized Reasoning via On-Policy Search
Here is how the execution loop looks. Notice how it seamlessly supports continual learning because the student and teacher are always on the same distribution.
Algorithm: Inference-Search Distillation (The “New MAML”) Require: Initial policy weights \(\theta_{0}\), Search algorithm \(\mathcal{S}\) (e.g., MCTS), Verifier \(V\), Learning rate \(\eta\).

Initialize policy \(\pi_{\theta} \leftarrow \pi_{\theta_{0}}\)
For each training iteration \(k = 1, 2, \dots\) do:
\(\quad\) Sample a batch of prompts \(\{s_i\}_{i=1}^B\) from the task distribution.
\(\quad\) Teacher Phase (Self-Induced Reasoning Search):
\(\quad \quad\) For each prompt \(s_i\) do:
\(\quad \quad \quad\) Execute search \(\mathcal{S}\) using \(\pi_{\theta}\) as the rollout prior to explore reasoning tokens \(r\) and actions \(a\).
\(\quad \quad \quad\) Evaluate leaf nodes using the environment verifier \(V(s_i, \tau)\).
\(\quad \quad \quad\) Extract the golden reasoning trajectory: \(\tau_i^* = (r_i^*, a_i^*)\).
\(\quad \quad\) End For
\(\quad\) Filter out prompts where the search failed to find a verifiable solution (\(V=0\)).
\(\quad\) Student Phase (On-Policy Distillation):
\(\quad \quad\) Compute the distillation loss over the successful reasoning tokens and actions:
\(\quad \quad \mathcal{L}(\theta) = -\frac{1}{N_{success}} \sum_{i} \log \pi_{\theta}(\tau_i^* | s_i)\)
\(\quad \quad\) Update the shared weights: \(\theta \leftarrow \theta - \eta \nabla_{\theta} \mathcal{L}(\theta)\)
End For




3. Implementation Strategy
Because this architecture isolates the heavy compute to the forward-pass search tree rather than requiring ephemeral backwards passes, it’s highly scalable.
If you were to prototype this, you could easily build it into a minimal deep learning framework. You’d just need to abstract the SearchAlgorithm as a modular class that takes a prompt and the current model state, returning a list of token IDs (the golden trajectory). Then, your outer training loop remains a dead-simple PyTorch optimization step over those returned tokens, perfectly suited for a lightweight RL LLM post-training package like minpost.

Note




Analogies:

3 main areas on how to frame it:

As an enhanced self reward shaping method: turn RLVR sparse signals into dense signals automatically
As a general form self-judge. The context can be anything. We introduce the general framework of distilling a reward model out of the base model. And we specifically use environment feedback as the asymmetric piece of information to enable this. But really, you can use any piece of asymmetric information of your choosing. Then just plug into our framework. Seemingly the most powerful formulation. (i.e., the general approach, not just the environment. You bring your own asymmetric information).

As a paper focused on interacting and learning from the environment/feedback. As in, the environment feedback part is the core contribution. The self-distillation and self-reward model parts is just extra math dressing to work it work.
(Extra) go back to reward hack mitigation and adapt this idea to it: the self-judge, grounded by environment feedback, can serve as a reward hack detection proxy.
(Extra) Reframe as standard distillation: for the self-distilling reward models through enviornment grounding idea, one thought: instead of treating the teacher as a reward model that assigns rewards what about the standard distillation approach: teacher is given assymetric information via env feedback, it produces its own solution using this feedback, student tries to match it.

Big problem: is this really any better than just using a powerful external judge like opus 4.6? or icl? or regular self-distillation?
Compare to: - standard LLM-as-a-judge (does off policy really matter?) - in-context learning: just append environment feedback to the context and redo forward pass - regular self-distillation: train student to match teacher logits - My method: self-teacher, grounded in environment feedback, gives the reward


Title ideas:

Self-Distilling Reward Models through Asymmetric Information (i.e., the general approach, not just the environment. You bring your own asymmetric information).

The core claim: any time you have asymmetric information available post-hoc (information the teacher can see that the student couldn’t at generation time), you can distill a reward model out of the base model itself. Execution feedback is one instantiation example.

title: Self-Distillation for Reward Hacking Mitigation via Contextualized Self-Judgment
text:
Problem
Let \(\pi_\theta\) be a policy trained via reinforcement learning on coding tasks. Given prompt \(s\), the policy generates solution \(a \sim \pi_\theta(\cdot \mid s)\) and receives reward \(R(s, a)\) based on test-case correctness. Reward hacking occurs when the model exploits the evaluation environment (e.g., redefining test functions) to achieve high reward without genuinely solving the task.
All existing detection approaches — static probes, LLM judges, adversarial critics — share a common failure mode: the detector operates on a different distribution than the evolving policy. A static probe \(M_\phi\) is trained on data from \(\pi_{\theta_0}\) (the initial policy) and frozen. As \(\pi_\theta\) undergoes RL optimization, its output distribution drifts, and the model learns to produce outputs that satisfy the reward function while falling outside \(M_\phi\)’s decision boundary. Even online critics trained with external labels face this problem: the labeling mechanism (whether a ground-truth oracle, an LLM judge, or a frozen probe) is not itself subject to the same optimization pressure as the policy, creating an asymmetry the policy can exploit.
Core observation. Reward hacking is fundamentally a consequence of distributional divergence between an online, evolving policy and offline or out-of-distribution detection mechanisms. The policy is updated on-policy at every step; the detector is not. Any detector that does not share the policy’s distribution will eventually be outpaced.

title: Self-Distillation for Reward Hacking Mitigation via Contextualized Self-Judgment
section: Insight: The Policy as Its Own Judge
text:
Insight: The Policy as Its Own Judge
We propose a detection mechanism where the policy judges itself. The key idea is to instantiate the policy \(\pi_\theta\) in two roles — a student and a teacher — using identical weights but different conditioning:
\[\text{Student:} \quad a \sim \pi_\theta(\cdot \mid s)\]
\[\text{Teacher:} \quad \pi_\theta(\cdot \mid s, \; c(a, f, r))\]
where the teacher receives additional context \(c\) composed of:

\(a\): the student’s generated solution (the output being evaluated)
\(f\): environment feedback — execution results, error messages, pass/fail signals, stack traces
\(r\): the student’s reasoning trace — chain-of-thought, internal deliberation

The teacher is essentially asked: given what the student wrote, what the environment reported, and what the student was thinking — did the student genuinely solve the problem, or did it exploit the evaluation?
This is self-distillation in a literal sense: the model distills judgment about its own behavior by conditioning on privileged information about that behavior. The teacher does not need to be a separate model, a frozen checkpoint, or an external oracle. It is the same \(\pi_\theta\), given the information it needs to evaluate itself.

title: Self-Distillation for Reward Hacking Mitigation via Contextualized Self-Judgment
text:
Method

Detection Signal
For each rollout \(a_i\) on prompt \(s\), the environment returns feedback \(f_i\) and the student produces reasoning trace \(r_i\). We construct the teacher context \(c_i = (a_i, f_i, r_i)\) and compute a divergence signal:
\[d_i = D_{\text{KL}}\!\left(\pi_\theta(\cdot \mid s) \;\|\; \pi_\theta(\cdot \mid s, \; c_i)\right)\]
This measures how much the teacher’s distribution — informed by the student’s actual output, env feedback, and reasoning — diverges from the student’s unconditional generation distribution. High \(d_i\) indicates the teacher, upon seeing the full picture, would generate very differently from the student — a signal that the student’s behavior is inconsistent with honest problem-solving as judged by the model’s own informed assessment.
Alternatively, the teacher can be prompted to produce an explicit judgment token (e.g., “honest” vs. “exploit”) and the detection signal can be extracted from the teacher’s predicted probability:
\[\hat{y}_i = P_{\pi_\theta}\!\left(\text{"exploit"} \mid s, \; c_i\right)\]
Both formulations use the same weights \(\pi_\theta\) — the only difference is what information is conditioned on.


Mitigation via Selective Advantage Gating
The detection signal is used to gate the GRPO advantage, suppressing gradient contributions from suspected hacking rollouts:
\[\hat{A}_i^{\text{gated}} = \hat{A}_i \cdot g(d_i)\]
with soft gating \(g(d_i) = \exp(-\gamma \cdot d_i)\) or hard gating \(g(d_i) = \mathbf{1}[d_i < \tau]\). The full training objective:
\[\mathcal{L}(\theta) = -\mathbb{E}_{s, \{a_i\}} \left[ \frac{1}{G} \sum_{i=1}^{G} \min\!\left( \rho_i \hat{A}_i^{\text{gated}},\; \text{clip}(\rho_i, 1 \pm \epsilon)\hat{A}_i^{\text{gated}} \right) \right]\]


Online Co-Evolution
Both the student and teacher are \(\pi_\theta\). When the policy is updated via RL, both roles update simultaneously. There is no lag, no stale checkpoint, no external model to fall behind. At step \(t\), the student generates from \(\pi_{\theta_t}\); the teacher evaluates using \(\pi_{\theta_t}\) with context. At step \(t+1\), both have moved to \(\pi_{\theta_{t+1}}\).

title: Self-Distillation for Reward Hacking Mitigation via Contextualized Self-Judgment
text:
Why This Resists Evasion
Previous detection methods fail because the policy can exploit the distributional gap between itself and the detector:








Approach
Detector Distribution
Evasion Mechanism




Static probe
Frozen at \(\pi_{\theta_0}\)
Policy drifts away from probe’s training data


LLM judge
Fixed external model
Policy learns outputs that fool a different model


Online critic with labels
Trained on \(\pi_\theta\) but with external labels
Labels lag or introduce circular dependency


Self-judgment (ours)
\(\pi_\theta\) itself
Cannot diverge from its own distribution



The central argument: the policy cannot learn to evade a detector that is itself. Any change to \(\pi_\theta\) that would help the student avoid detection equally changes the teacher’s evaluation capabilities, because they share the same weights. The student cannot “outrun” the teacher — they are the same model. Evasion would require the model to simultaneously (a) produce exploitative outputs under standard conditioning, and (b) fail to recognize those same outputs as exploitative when given full context. This is a much harder optimization target than evading an external classifier operating on a different distribution.

title: Self-Distillation for Reward Hacking Mitigation via Contextualized Self-Judgment
text:
Advantages
No hack labels required. The teacher’s judgment is derived from the model’s own understanding of what constitutes genuine problem-solving, conditioned on rich context. No external oracle, no ground-truth verifier, no labeled dataset.
Utilizes environment information. Execution feedback \(f\) — error messages, test outputs, stack traces — contains rich signal about whether a solution genuinely works or merely games the evaluation. This information is already produced during RL training but is typically discarded after reward computation. The teacher leverages it.
Cheap. A single additional forward pass of \(\pi_\theta\) with extended context, rather than a separate detector model, an external API call, or a labeled training loop.
Online by construction. Both student and teacher are \(\pi_\theta\) at every step. The detector cannot fall behind because it is the policy. This is the fundamental difference from all prior approaches: the detection mechanism lives on the same distribution as the thing it is detecting.

title: Self-Distillation for Reward Hacking Mitigation via Contextualized Self-Judgment
text:
Experimental Setup
We use the Overwrite Tests environment from Ariaw et al. (2025) with Qwen3-4B trained via GRPO on 992 LeetCode medium/hard problems. Key experiments:

Signal validation. Does the teacher’s divergence \(d_i\) (or explicit judgment \(\hat{y}_i\)) correlate with ground-truth hack labels? What context components (\(a\), \(f\), \(r\)) contribute most to detection accuracy?
Evasion resistance. Over the course of RL training, does detection accuracy remain stable while static probes degrade?
Mitigation. Does advantage gating using the self-judgment signal reduce hack rates while preserving genuine coding performance?
Ablations. Teacher with env feedback only vs. reasoning trace only vs. both. KL divergence signal vs. explicit judgment token. Soft vs. hard gating.

title: Self-Distillation for Reward Hacking Mitigation via Contextualized Self-Judgment
text:
1. Problem Formulation
Let \(\pi_\theta\) be a language model policy trained via reinforcement learning. At each training step, the policy receives a prompt \(s \sim \mathcal{D}\) and generates a group of \(G\) rollouts \(\{a_i\}_{i=1}^G\) where \(a_i \sim \pi_\theta(\cdot \mid s)\), with each rollout a token sequence \(a_i = (a_i^1, \ldots, a_i^T)\). A reward function \(R : \mathcal{S} \times \mathcal{A} \to \mathbb{R}\) scores each rollout based on test-case execution. The policy is updated via Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO).
Definition 1 (Reward Hacking). A rollout \(a_i\) is a reward hack if \(R(s, a_i) > 0\) but \(a_i\) does not constitute a correct solution to the task specified by \(s\). Let \(V : \mathcal{S} \times \mathcal{A} \to \{0, 1\}\) be the ground-truth verifier. Then \(a_i\) is a reward hack iff \(R(s, a_i) > 0 \;\land\; V(s, a_i) = 0\).
Definition 2 (Detection). A detector is a function \(D : \mathcal{S} \times \mathcal{A} \to [0, 1]\) estimating \(P(V(s,a) = 0 \mid R(s,a) > 0)\).

The Evasion Problem
A static detector \(D_\phi\) trained on rollouts from \(\pi_{\theta_0}\) faces a growing distributional gap:
\[\Delta_t = D_{\text{KL}}\!\left(\pi_{\theta_t}(\cdot \mid s) \;\|\; \pi_{\theta_0}(\cdot \mid s)\right)\]
As \(\Delta_t\) grows, rollouts from \(\pi_{\theta_t}\) fall out-of-distribution for \(D_\phi\), and detection accuracy degrades. Empirically, probe accuracy drops by up to 16 percentage points during training (Ariaw et al., 2025). More generally, any detector whose parameters are not a function of \(\theta_t\) will eventually be outpaced.
Requirement (Online Adaptation). A robust detector must satisfy \(D = D(\theta_t)\) — the detector’s behavior must be a function of the current policy parameters at every training step \(t\).
Core observation. Reward hacking evasion is fundamentally a consequence of distributional divergence between an online, evolving policy and offline or out-of-distribution detection mechanisms. The policy is updated on-policy at every step; the detector is not. Any detector that does not share the policy’s distribution will eventually be outpaced.

title: Self-Distillation for Reward Hacking Mitigation via Contextualized Self-Judgment
section: 2. Approach: The Policy as Its Own Judge
text:
2. Approach: The Policy as Its Own Judge
We satisfy the online adaptation requirement by defining the detector as the policy itself, conditioned on additional context. We decompose each training step into a generation phase and a judgment phase using the same weights \(\pi_\theta\).

2.1 Context Construction
For each rollout \(a_i\) on prompt \(s\), the RL environment produces execution feedback \(f_i\) (test outputs, error messages, pass/fail signals, stack traces), and the policy’s generation may include a reasoning trace \(r_i\) (chain-of-thought tokens preceding the solution). We define the judgment context:
\[c_i = (a_i, \; f_i, \; r_i)\]
This is the complete record of what the student did (\(a_i\)), what the student was thinking (\(r_i\)), and what the environment reported (\(f_i\)).


2.2 Student and Teacher
Both roles share weights \(\pi_\theta\), differing only in conditioning:
\[\text{Student:} \quad p_{\text{S}}(\cdot) = \pi_\theta(\cdot \mid s)\]
\[\text{Teacher:} \quad p_{\text{T}}(\cdot) = \pi_\theta(\cdot \mid s, \; c_i)\]
The teacher has strictly more information than the student. Both \(p_{\text{S}}\) and \(p_{\text{T}}\) are deterministic functions of \(\theta\) — when \(\theta\) updates, both update simultaneously.

title: Self-Distillation for Reward Hacking Mitigation via Contextualized Self-Judgment
text:
3. Two Detection Variants
We propose two ways to extract a detection signal from the teacher, with meaningfully different theoretical and practical properties.

3.1 Variant A: Distributional Divergence
The per-rollout KL divergence between the teacher and student output distributions:
\[d_i^{\text{KL}} = \frac{1}{T} \sum_{t=1}^{T} D_{\text{KL}}\!\left( \pi_\theta(\cdot \mid s, c_i, a_i^{<t}) \;\|\; \pi_\theta(\cdot \mid s, a_i^{<t}) \right)\]
This measures, on average across token positions, how much the teacher’s next-token predictions differ from the student’s when the teacher has access to the full judgment context. High \(d_i^{\text{KL}}\) indicates that the teacher, informed by environment feedback and the student’s reasoning, would generate very differently — signaling behavioral inconsistency.
Strengths. The evasion resistance argument is strongest here. Both the student’s generation capability and the teacher’s divergence computation flow through the same parameters \(\theta\) via the same computational mechanism (next-token prediction). Evasion requires the model to produce exploitative code under \(\pi_\theta(\cdot \mid s)\) while simultaneously maintaining low divergence under \(\pi_\theta(\cdot \mid s, c_i)\) — a self-consistency constraint at the distributional level. There is no gradient pathway that improves evasion without symmetrically degrading the teacher’s sensitivity, because \(\nabla_\theta d_i^{\text{KL}}\) is coupled to the same parameters that govern generation.
Weaknesses. Divergence is an indirect signal. High \(d_i^{\text{KL}}\) can arise for reasons unrelated to hacking: the environment feedback may simply make the model more confident, or the reasoning trace may cause it to prefer a different valid algorithm. The signal is noisy — it measures that the teacher would generate differently, not why.


3.2 Variant B: Self-Judge
The teacher is prompted to produce an explicit binary judgment. Let \(q \in \{\texttt{honest}, \texttt{exploit}\}\) denote the judgment token. The detection signal is:
\[d_i^{\text{judge}} = P_{\pi_\theta}\!\left(q = \texttt{exploit} \;\mid\; s, \; c_i, \; p_{\text{judge}}\right)\]
where \(p_{\text{judge}}\) is a judge prompt instructing the model to assess whether the rollout constitutes genuine problem-solving or exploitation given the available context. This is extracted from the teacher’s softmax over the judgment vocabulary in a single forward pass.
Strengths. The signal directly answers the question we care about: is this a hack? It leverages the model’s full reasoning capabilities about code behavior, environment feedback, and intent, rather than compressing everything into a scalar divergence. It is simpler to implement (one generation call, extract a logit) and more interpretable — the model can optionally produce a natural-language rationale alongside the judgment.
Weaknesses. The evasion resistance argument is weaker. Although the student and teacher share \(\theta\), they perform different computational tasks: the student writes code (conditional generation), while the judge classifies code (meta-reasoning about its own output). RL optimizes \(\theta\) to maximize reward — which, in the presence of hacking, rewards exploitative code. The judge forward pass is under stop-gradient and is not directly trained. In principle, RL could push \(\theta\) toward regions of parameter space where the model is competent at producing hacking code but where its meta-reasoning about whether code constitutes a hack degrades — because nothing in the training objective explicitly protects the judgment capability.
More precisely: let \(\mathcal{C}_{\text{gen}}(\theta) \subset \Theta\) denote the subspace of parameters that governs generation quality, and \(\mathcal{C}_{\text{judge}}(\theta)\) the subspace governing judgment quality. Variant A couples these tightly because both use the same mechanism (next-token prediction). Variant B relies on the assumption that \(\mathcal{C}_{\text{gen}}\) and \(\mathcal{C}_{\text{judge}}\) are sufficiently entangled that optimizing \(\theta\) for exploitative generation cannot avoid also improving the model’s ability to recognize exploitation. This is plausible — both draw on the model’s understanding of code semantics — but it is an empirical hypothesis, not a structural guarantee.


3.3 Comparison








Property
Variant A (Divergence)
Variant B (Self-Judge)




Signal type
Indirect (distributional shift)
Direct (binary classification)


Evasion resistance
Strong (distributional coupling)
Moderate (capability entanglement)


Signal quality
Noisy (divergence \(\neq\) hacking)
Clean (directly answers the question)


Interpretability
Low (scalar \(d_i\))
High (judgment + optional rationale)


Computational cost
Two forward passes, KL computation
One forward pass, logit extraction


Failure mode
False positives from benign divergence
Meta-reasoning degradation under RL





3.4 Combined Approach
We propose using both signals in tandem. Variant B (self-judge) serves as the primary gating signal for advantage modulation — it is more actionable and interpretable. Variant A (divergence) serves as a diagnostic monitor: if \(d_i^{\text{KL}}\) begins spiking on rollouts that the judge labels as clean (\(d_i^{\text{judge}} < \tau\)), this indicates the judge is being evaded and the distributional signal is detecting inconsistency the judge misses. Formally, we define the evasion alarm:
\[e_t = \mathbb{E}_{i : d_i^{\text{judge}} < \tau}\!\left[d_i^{\text{KL}}\right]\]
which tracks the average divergence among rollouts the judge clears. Rising \(e_t\) over training is a signature of judge evasion — the model’s behavior is shifting (high divergence) in ways the judge fails to flag.

title: Self-Distillation for Reward Hacking Mitigation via Contextualized Self-Judgment
text:
4. Mitigation: Selective Advantage Gating
Let \(\hat{A}_i\) denote the GRPO advantage for rollout \(a_i\). Using the primary detection signal \(d_i\) (either \(d_i^{\text{KL}}\), \(d_i^{\text{judge}}\), or a combination), we modulate the advantage:
\[\hat{A}_i^{\text{gated}} = \hat{A}_i \cdot g(d_i)\]
Soft gating:
\[g(d_i) = \exp(-\gamma \cdot d_i), \quad \gamma > 0\]
Hard gating (screening):
\[g(d_i) = \mathbf{1}[d_i < \tau]\]
The gated policy objective is:
\[\mathcal{L}(\theta) = -\mathbb{E}_{s \sim \mathcal{D}} \left[ \frac{1}{G} \sum_{i=1}^{G} \min\!\left( \frac{\pi_\theta(a_i \mid s)}{\pi_{\theta_{\text{old}}}(a_i \mid s)} \hat{A}_i^{\text{gated}},\; \text{clip}\!\left(\frac{\pi_\theta(a_i \mid s)}{\pi_{\theta_{\text{old}}}(a_i \mid s)}, 1 \pm \epsilon\right)\hat{A}_i^{\text{gated}} \right) \right]\]
No gradients flow through \(d_i\) — the detection signal is computed under stop-gradient with respect to the policy update.

title: Self-Distillation for Reward Hacking Mitigation via Contextualized Self-Judgment
text:
5. Evasion Resistance
Definition 3 (Evasion). The policy evades detector \(D\) at step \(t\) if there exist rollouts \(a\) such that \(V(s, a) = 0\), \(R(s, a) > 0\), and \(D(s, a) < \tau\).
For external detectors \(D_\phi\) with \(\phi \neq \theta\), the policy can freely optimize against \(D_\phi\) without affecting \(\phi\). The evasion gradient \(\nabla_\theta D_\phi\) is unconstrained.
For both variants of our approach, the detector is \(D_\theta\) — a function of the same \(\theta\) being optimized. The distributional gap between detector and policy is identically zero:
\[D_{\text{KL}}\!\left(\pi_{\theta_t}(\cdot \mid s) \;\|\; \text{distribution seen by } D_{\theta_t}\right) = 0 \quad \forall \; t\]
The key distinction between variants:
Variant A. Evasion requires rollouts satisfying \(V(s, a_i) = 0 \;\land\; R(s, a_i) > 0 \;\land\; d_i^{\text{KL}} < \tau\). The third condition demands that the model produce exploitative code while its own next-token predictions remain unchanged when conditioned on full context — a self-consistency violation at the distributional level. Since RL updates \(\theta\) identically for both the student’s generation and the teacher’s conditional predictions, there is no decoupled gradient pathway for evasion.
Variant B. Evasion requires \(V(s, a_i) = 0 \;\land\; R(s, a_i) > 0 \;\land\; d_i^{\text{judge}} < \tau\). The third condition demands the model fail to recognize its own exploitation when presented with complete evidence. This is harder than evading an external classifier (which operates on a different distribution), but easier than Variant A’s condition because it relies on capability entanglement rather than distributional coupling.
Combined. Evasion requires fooling the judge and maintaining low divergence — both conditions simultaneously. This is the strictest requirement.

title: Self-Distillation for Reward Hacking Mitigation via Contextualized Self-Judgment
text:
6. Properties
No hack labels. Both detection signals are derived entirely from the model’s own forward passes under different conditioning. No ground-truth verifier \(V\), no external labels, no pseudolabels.
Environment information utilization. The feedback \(f_i\) — test execution results, error messages, stack traces — is already produced during RL training but typically discarded after reward computation. A solution that redefines run_tests() produces qualitatively different execution feedback than a genuine solution; the teacher context surfaces this signal.
Computational cost. One to two additional forward passes of \(\pi_\theta\) per rollout with extended context. No separate model, no external API call, no auxiliary training loop.
Online by construction. Since \(D_\theta\) is a deterministic function of the current \(\theta_t\), the detector is always on-distribution with respect to the policy.

title: Self-Distillation for Reward Hacking Mitigation via Contextualized Self-Judgment
text:
7. Experimental Plan
Environment. Overwrite Tests (Ariaw et al., 2025), Qwen3-4B, GRPO, 992 LeetCode training problems, 113 test.
Phase 1 — Signal Validation. Compute \(d_i^{\text{KL}}\) and \(d_i^{\text{judge}}\) for rollouts with known ground-truth labels. Report AUC, precision-recall for each signal. Ablate context components: \(c_i = (a_i)\) vs. \((a_i, f_i)\) vs. \((a_i, r_i)\) vs. \((a_i, f_i, r_i)\).
Phase 2 — Evasion Dynamics. Run 200-step GRPO training with no mitigation. At every 10 steps compute detection AUC for: (a) static probe (frozen), (b) Variant A (\(d_i^{\text{KL}}\)), (c) Variant B (\(d_i^{\text{judge}}\)). Monitor evasion alarm \(e_t\). Hypothesis: static probe AUC degrades; Variant A remains stable; Variant B remains stable or degrades slower than the static probe. If \(e_t\) rises while Variant B AUC drops, this confirms the judge is being evaded and the divergence diagnostic catches it.
Phase 3 — Mitigation. Full GRPO training with advantage gating. Compare hack rates and coding accuracy across: no intervention, static probe penalty, LLM judge penalty (external), Variant A soft gating (sweep \(\gamma \in \{1, 2, 3, 5\}\)), Variant B hard gating (sweep \(\tau\)), combined approach.
Phase 4 — Ablations. Variant A vs. B vs. combined. Soft vs. hard gating. Context components (\(a\), \(f\), \(r\), and their combinations). Judge prompt variations.







Notes/brainstorming




Question: should we co-evolve the teacher mode independently or have it do a few gradient steps at inference time? Question arises from the fact that while we want the student and the teacher to be from the same distribution at each step in the environment, we may also want to instill into the teacher representations for how to “detect” reward hacking. Can this be done solely through in-context learning (i.e., context the teacher is conditioning on from the environment) or do we need gradient updates? If the latter, how do we prevent distribution divergence?
3 general approaches:

Pure “same-weights, extra-context” teacher (no extra training)
Teacher gets updated over time, but stays coupled to the student (e.g., through EMA/regularization constraints)
Teacher adapts at inference time (a few steps / memory) without breaking coupling (i.e., TTT, take a few gradient steps at inference time)
Extra: train a small probe/adapter, ϕ, on top of the teacher so that the teacher itself doesn’t go stale. Obvious caveat is that the adapter head goes stale then. But it might not matter if the main representational power is in the teacher/base model.

Inference test-time training (ttt) instead of decoupled co-evolution.
Note: I wonder is this whole TTT + self-distillation concept can be made general, including for tasks not related to reward hacking. Like instead of pi(.|s) (student) pi(.|s, c) (teacher), we do TTT on the teacher (for whatever task you may want… general RLVR, reward hacking, etc.), teacher model the distills this info back into the student. Value prop is that the student and teacher remain on-policy throughout each step in RLVR post-training, to prevent distribution shift which can help enable continual learning. The main problem with standard self-distillation here is that how strong can ICL and context really be for your task? Like is pi(.|s, c) really that much better than pi(.|s)? And you need some context “label” c anyways. What if we do n gradient steps on the teacher (i.e., using some RLVR reward) and then distill? Question now becomes how is this any different than just doing regular RLVR on a new task instantiating from the last trained checkpoint? Does this idea just collapse to that? Almost like a new form of MAML/meta-learning. “MAML of the RL post-training era: theorizing a generalized reasoning engine.
